proposals.title: Re-evaluating the 'Typical' Range of Indoor Ozone Concentrations: A Meta-Analysis and Field Study to Assess Representativeness for Human Exposure and Skin Chemistry Studies
proposals.motivation: Much of the foundational research on ozone-initiated skin chemistry, squalene oxidation, and personal cloud formation has been conducted at controlled, often elevated, ozone concentrations (e.g., 30-80 ppb) to elicit measurable effects. However, the representativeness of these levels for typical residential and office environments globally is unclear. If commonly studied concentrations are atypically high due to specific local factors (e.g., high outdoor levels, certain HVAC operations), the derived health implications and reaction rates may not accurately reflect real-world human exposure and indoor air chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: The concentration ranges of indoor ozone commonly used in controlled chamber studies and cited as 'typical' in the literature are skewed towards the upper end of the distribution, influenced by data from regions with high outdoor ozone and specific building types, and thus may overestimate population-level exposure and associated chemical dynamics at the skin surface.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic meta-analysis of peer-reviewed literature (pre-2026) reporting measured indoor ozone concentrations in North America, Europe, and Asia, categorizing data by building type, season, ventilation, and geographic region. This will be supplemented with a new field campaign deploying passive ozone samplers in a stratified random sample of 100 homes and offices in three climatically distinct U.S. cities to capture 'background' conditions. Statistical analysis (kernel density estimation, ANOVA) will compare the compiled real-world distribution against the concentration ranges typically used in controlled human exposure and surface chemistry studies from Weschler's and other labs.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, geographically representative baseline for 'normal' indoor ozone levels, against which experimental conditions can be contextualized. It will allow for a more accurate, population-weighted assessment of the public health significance of ozone-skin chemistry and the potential impact of interventions like UVC lights or air cleaners that alter indoor ozone concentrations.
proposals.novelty: While many studies measure indoor ozone, none have systematically critiqued whether the experimental levels driving a major subfield (indoor human chemistry) are representative. This directly questions the external validity of a substantial body of work by linking large-scale exposure science to mechanistic chamber studies.

proposals.title: Linking Indoor Ozone-Driven Skin Chemistry to Specific Respiratory Irritants: A Controlled Human Exposure and Air Monitoring Study
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies consistently link higher ventilation rates with improved respiratory health, but the specific chemical agents mediating this relationship remain poorly characterized. Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others demonstrates that ozone-initiated reactions with skin lipids like squalene generate a complex mixture of volatile and semi-volatile organic compounds (VOCs and SVOCs), some of which are known irritants. Identifying which of these reaction products are the key causative agents for health effects is critical for refining indoor air quality guidelines and targeted mitigation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific secondary organic aerosols (SOAs) and high-yield carbonyl VOCs (e.g., 6-methyl-5-hepten-2-one, geranyl acetone) generated from the ozonolysis of human skin oil are primary mediators of the observed respiratory irritation linked to indoor ozone exposure and ventilation conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled, small-scale human exposure studies in a climate-controlled chamber with adjustable ozone levels (0-40 ppb). Human volunteers, wearing standardized clothing, will be exposed while real-time instruments (PTR-TOF-MS, SMPS, NOx/O3 monitors) quantify gas- and particle-phase products. Simultaneously, we will collect detailed symptom reports and measure exhaled breath condensate for biomarkers of oxidative stress and inflammation (e.g., 8-isoprostane, IL-6). Statistical mediation analysis and positive matrix factorization (PMF) will be used to correlate specific chemical species or factor profiles with acute physiological responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond correlating ventilation rates with health outcomes to establish a mechanistic, exposure-driven link between a defined indoor chemistry pathway and measurable biological effects. It will identify priority pollutant classes for future sensor development and filtration standards. The findings will provide a chemical basis for personal exposure mitigation, such as selecting skincare products that minimize reactive lipid content.
proposals.novelty: While the generation of compounds from skin-ozone reactions is known, directly linking specific products to acute human health responses in a controlled setting is novel. This study integrates real-time, high-resolution analytical chemistry with concurrent, detailed human symptomology and biomarkers to pinpoint causative agents, a significant advance over population-level association studies.

proposals.title: Kinetic Limitations in Gas-Particle Partitioning of Ozone-Derived Skin Oil Oxidation Products: Implications for Indoor Aerosol Composition and Exposure
proposals.motivation: Indoor air chemistry models often assume instantaneous equilibrium for volatile organic compounds (VOCs) partitioning to aerosols, a simplification that may misrepresent the dynamic indoor environment. For ozone-skin oil reactions, which generate a complex mixture of semi- and low-volatility products (e.g., from squalene), kinetic limitations could significantly alter predicted aerosol-phase concentrations and composition. This is critical for accurate exposure assessment, as these particles can carry reactive species and influence respiratory health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the partitioning of key, reactive oxidation products from the ozonolysis of skin oil constituents (like squalene) into indoor aerosols is kinetically limited under realistic indoor residential and office conditions, leading to systematically different aerosol-phase concentrations and oxidant burdens compared to equilibrium model predictions.
proposals.method: We will develop a kinetic multilayer model for gas-particle partitioning (KM-GAP) coupled to a detailed chemical mechanism for ozone-skin oil surface reactions. The model will treat mass transport and chemical reactions in distinct layers: gas phase, a surface sorption layer, and the aerosol bulk. We will parameterize the model using pre-2026 experimental data on squalene ozonolysis product yields and physicochemical properties (vapor pressure, diffusivity). The model will be applied to simulate time-varying scenarios informed by real human occupancy, activity, and ventilation patterns from prior chamber and field studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first quantitative framework for assessing non-equilibrium partitioning of skin-derived oxidation products in indoor air. It will yield new insights into the transient nature of personal cloud chemistry and improve the mechanistic understanding of how human occupants influence the composition and toxicity of indoor aerosols. The model will be a valuable tool for more accurate exposure assessment in health studies.
proposals.novelty: While kinetic partitioning models exist for atmospheric aerosols, their application to the unique, human-influenced, and transient chemical system of indoor air—specifically for reactive skin oil products—is novel. This moves beyond the standard equilibrium assumption prevalent in indoor air quality modeling prior to 2026.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Relative Contribution of Radical vs. Non-Radical Oxidation Pathways for Ozone-Skin Oil Reactions in Simulated Indoor Droplets
proposals.motivation: Indoor ozone reacts with skin lipids like squalene on surfaces and in airborne particles, generating a complex mixture of oxidation products that impact indoor air quality and human health. While photochemically-initiated radical pathways are considered dominant, the potential for direct, thermal ozonolysis and other non-radical pathways in microenvironments like skin oil-laden droplets is poorly quantified. Understanding the competition between these pathways is crucial for accurately modeling indoor oxidative chemistry and predicting human exposure to secondary pollutants.
proposals.hypothesis: In indoor-relevant aqueous droplets containing skin oil components, non-radical (thermal) oxidation pathways, particularly direct ozonolysis, compete significantly with photochemically-initiated radical-mediated oxidation for key substrates like squalene, especially under low-light or dark conditions typical of many indoor spaces.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled chamber experiments using atomized droplets of synthetic skin oil mixtures (squalene, oleic acid, cholesterol) in water/glycerol matrices. Experiments will systematically vary conditions: with/without UVC/UV-A light (to toggle photochemical radical initiation), ozone concentration (10-80 ppb), relative humidity (20-60%), and droplet pH. Product speciation and yields will be analyzed via TD-GC-MS and HPLC-MS. The relative contribution of pathways will be quantified by comparing product ratios (e.g., 6-MHO vs. other squalene ozonolysis products) and kinetics in light vs. dark scenarios, and by using radical scavengers (e.g., 2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol) to suppress radical chain reactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental quantification of the competition between radical and non-radical oxidative pathways for a key indoor substrate in a droplet phase. It will refine fundamental chemical mechanisms for indoor models and clarify how lighting conditions and droplet properties modulate the chemical fate of ozone indoors. The results will inform exposure assessments by identifying which oxidation products are likely to form under specific indoor environmental conditions.
proposals.novelty: While Weschler's prior work has characterized the ozone-skin oil system and the impact of light, it has not explicitly deconvoluted and quantified the contribution of thermal, non-radical pathways versus photochemical radical routes within the physically distinct and relevant droplet phase, where chemistry may differ from bulk surfaces or the gas phase.

proposals.title: Catalytic Roles of Transition Metals in Indoor Aerosol Chemistry: Linking Human Emissions, Ozone, and Reactive Oxygen Species
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone reacts with skin lipids like squalene on surfaces and in the gas phase, generating a complex mixture of volatile and semi-volatile organic compounds (VOCs and SVOCs). These products can partition into indoor aqueous aerosols, which contain trace transition metals (e.g., Cu, Mn, V) from outdoor infiltration and indoor sources. The catalytic redox cycles of these metals in aerosols are poorly characterized but are hypothesized to be a major, overlooked source of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and secondary organic aerosol (SOA) indoors, with direct implications for inhalation exposure and respiratory health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that copper, manganese, and vanadium, dissolved in indoor aqueous aerosols, catalyze sustained Fenton-like and Haber-Weiss reactions, converting ozone-derived organic peroxides and hydroperoxides into highly oxidizing radicals (e.g., OH), thereby amplifying oxidative stress in the respiratory tract and altering the chemical lifetime of key indoor pollutants.
proposals.method: We will generate model indoor aerosols in a controlled environmental chamber by ozonolysis of squalene and other skin oil surrogates in the presence of human volunteers or simulated skin surfaces. Aerosols will be collected onto PTFE filters and aqueous suspensions will be analyzed for metal content (ICP-MS) and speciation (XANES). Catalytic activity will be quantified by measuring ROS generation (e.g., hydroxyl radical via fluorescent probe assays) and the decay of probe organic compounds (e.g., methoxybenzene) in synthesized aerosol mimics with controlled metal chemistry. A kinetic model, incorporating established aqueous-phase radical mechanisms (e.g., from the CAPRAM framework) and metal redox cycles, will be developed to interpret results and extrapolate to real indoor settings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for transition metal catalysis in indoor aerosols, directly linking human chemistry and outdoor-origin ozone to sustained indoor radical generation. It will identify the most catalytically significant metals and their redox partners, offering a mechanistic explanation for observed oxidative potential of indoor PM. The results will inform exposure assessments and mitigation strategies for sensitive populations.
proposals.novelty: While metal catalysis is studied in atmospheric aerosols, its specific role in the unique chemical milieu of indoor air—rich in skin-derived, unsaturated organics from direct human emission—is virtually unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates Weschler's expertise in human-influenced indoor chemistry with established aqueous-phase atmospheric chemistry methods to address a critical indoor-specific knowledge gap.

proposals.title: Stopped-Flow Kinetic Analysis of Aqueous-Phase Radical Reactions in Model Indoor Aerosols: From Skin Oil Ozonolysis to Secondary Organic Aerosol Formation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Weschler's own work, has established that ozone-initiated reactions with skin lipids like squalene generate gas-phase products and secondary organic aerosols (SOA) indoors, impacting indoor air quality and potential health effects. However, the kinetics of the fast aqueous-phase radical reactions (e.g., involving Criegee intermediates or OH radicals) within microscopic aqueous aerosol droplets or on skin oil films remain poorly quantified due to experimental challenges in measuring rapid mixing and reaction timescales. This gap limits our ability to model the real-time chemical evolution of indoor aerosols and their interaction with the human oxidation field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying stopped-flow rapid-mixing techniques will enable the direct measurement of second-order rate constants for key aqueous-phase radical reactions (e.g., OH oxidation of squalene oxidation products) under indoor-relevant conditions, and that these kinetics are significantly faster than currently estimated from bulk-phase studies.
proposals.method: We will adapt a commercial stopped-flow spectrophotometer, modifying mixing syringes to handle organic-aqueous emulsions simulating skin oil films and indoor aerosols. One syringe will contain a solution of a target organic compound (e.g., 6-methyl-5-hepten-2-one, a major squalene ozonolysis product) in a pH-buffered aqueous matrix, while the other will contain a rapidly generated radical source (e.g., OH from H2O2 photolysis via an integrated pulsed LED). Reaction progress will be monitored via UV-Vis absorbance changes of specific chromophores or by coupling to a rapid-quenching device for offline analysis via HPLC-MS. Temperature and humidity will be controlled to mimic indoor environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first directly measured, high-precision kinetic parameters for crucial aqueous-phase radical reactions involving indoor-relevant organics. It will significantly improve the mechanistic fidelity of indoor air chemistry models, particularly for predicting SOA formation from human occupancy. The data will also inform understanding of the chemical lifetime of reactive species on skin and in respiratory tract lining fluid, bridging surface chemistry and health.
proposals.novelty: While stopped-flow is standard in biochemistry, its application to quantify kinetics in heterogeneous, indoor-relevant aerosol systems is novel. This approach directly tackles the 'mixing problem' in micro-droplets, moving beyond bulk solution kinetics to provide data on timescales previously inaccessible for indoor aerosol chemistry.

proposals.title: Squalene Ozonolysis in Skin Oil Aerosols: A Novel Pathway for Indoor Synthesis of Low-Volatility Organic Compounds
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone reacts with skin-sourced squalene on indoor surfaces and in the gas phase, generating a range of oxidation products. However, the role of squalene-containing skin oil droplets, suspended as aerosols from human activity, as unique micro-reactors for condensed-phase organic synthesis remains unexplored. This gap is significant because such aerosols could be a major, previously unrecognized source of secondary organic aerosols (SOA) and semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs) in occupied indoor environments, with implications for indoor air quality and human exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that squalene-rich skin oil aerosols, generated under realistic indoor conditions, will act as efficient micro-reactors for ozonolysis, producing a distinct suite of low-volatility organic compounds (LVOCs) and secondary organic aerosol mass at rates exceeding those predicted by gas-surface or gas-phase reactions alone.
proposals.method: We will generate reproducible skin oil aerosol proxies (e.g., squalene, squalane, triolein mixtures) using a Collison nebulizer within a 2 m³ Teflon reaction chamber under controlled temperature (25-30°C) and humidity (30-70% RH). Ozone will be introduced at indoor-relevant concentrations (20-50 ppb). Aerosol size distribution and mass concentration will be monitored in real-time using a Scanning Mobility Particle Sizer (SMPS). Particle-phase products will be collected on Teflon filters and quartz fiber filters for detailed chemical analysis via Thermal Desorption–Gas Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry (TD-GC-MS) and Direct Analysis in Real Time–Mass Spectrometry (DART-MS) to identify and quantify reaction products.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, human-driven source mechanism for indoor aerosols via skin oil ozonolysis in the aerosol phase. It will provide the first quantitative yield data for LVOC/SOA formation from this pathway, directly informing indoor air quality models. The findings will bridge Weschler's prior work on surface chemistry and the human oxidation field to the dynamic aerosol phase, offering a more complete picture of personal cloud chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While squalene-ozone reaction pathways are known, treating skin oil aerosols as deliberate, confined micro-reactors for organic synthesis under indoor conditions is a novel conceptual framework. The experimental approach uniquely isolates the aerosol-phase process from competing surface-mediated pathways, a distinction not made in prior indoor chemistry studies.

proposals.title: Kinetics of Ozone-Skin Oil Chemistry Under Extreme Humidity Cycling: Implications for Indoor Aerosol Dynamics and Personal Exposure
proposals.motivation: Indoor humidity undergoes rapid, large-scale fluctuations due to activities like cooking, showering, and HVAC operation. Pre-2026 research has established that ozone reacts with skin oil constituents like squalene on surfaces and in the air, forming semi-volatile and low-volatility organic compounds (SVOCs and LVOCs) that partition into aerosols. However, the chemical kinetics of these reactions during dynamic humidity cycles, which drive aerosol growth and evaporation, remain unexplored, creating a critical gap in predicting personal exposure to oxidation products.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rapid humidity cycling will non-linearly accelerate the ozonolysis of skin oil compounds by modulating water availability on surfaces and in condensed aerosol phases, thereby altering the yield and partitioning behavior of key reaction products such as 6-methyl-5-hepten-2-one (6-MHO) and geranyl acetone.
proposals.method: We will employ a controlled environmental chamber coupled with a fast-humidity modulation system (using dry/steam injection) to simulate extreme cycles (e.g., 20% to 80% RH over minutes). A model skin surface (squalene-coated glass or fabric) and generated squalene aerosols will be exposed to realistic indoor ozone levels (~20 ppb). Real-time product monitoring will use a Proton-Transfer-Reaction Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometer (PTR-TOF-MS) and a Scanning Mobility Particle Sizer (SMPS). Kinetic modeling will be performed using the GECKO-A (Generator of Explicit Chemistry and Kinetics of Organics in the Atmosphere) explicit mechanism generator, adapted for condensed-phase and surface reactions, to interpret the humidity-dependent rate constants and product yields.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first kinetic parameters for key indoor oxidation pathways under realistic, transient humidity conditions. It will directly inform indoor air quality and exposure models by quantifying the dynamic source strength of SVOCs from human chemistry, thereby improving risk assessments for respiratory and dermal irritation linked to secondary organic aerosols.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies by Weschler and others have characterized skin oil ozonolysis at steady-state humidity, this proposal uniquely investigates the kinetic forcing function of rapid humidity cycling, integrating surface, aerosol, and gas-phase chemistry in a single dynamic framework to resolve time-dependent product formation and partitioning.

proposals.title: Modeling Population-Level Exposure to Ozone-Derived Skin Lipid Oxidation Products in the U.S. Residential Building Stock
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone reacts with skin surface lipids like squalene, generating potentially harmful oxidation products (e.g., aldehydes, ketones) in the breathing zone. While chamber studies quantify these processes, the population-level exposure burden remains unknown. This is a critical gap for indoor air quality and public health, as most people spend the majority of their time indoors where ozone and skin oil coexist.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating mechanistic models of skin surface ozonolysis with national housing stock data will reveal significant variability in population exposure to oxidation products, driven by building characteristics, climate, and occupant density.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic exposure model by coupling a mass-balance indoor chemistry module (based on Weschler's established models for skin oil ozonolysis) with the U.S. Residential Energy Consumption Survey (RECS) database. The model will stochastically simulate indoor environments defined by RECS (e.g., volume, air exchange, ozone penetration) and occupant activity. Key parameters (skin surface area, squalene emission, reaction probability) will be drawn from pre-2026 literature and assigned via Monte Carlo simulation to generate a national distribution of personal exposure concentrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first national-scale estimate of exposure to ozone-initiated skin chemistry products, identifying vulnerable subpopulations based on housing type and region. It will translate mechanistic chemistry into a public health context, offering a framework for integrating building science and indoor chemistry in exposure assessment.
proposals.novelty: While building stock models exist for traditional pollutants (e.g., PM2.5, radon), none have incorporated the dynamic, person-centric source of skin oil oxidation. This approach uniquely scales a fundamentally personal chemical process to a population level using nationally representative building data.

proposals.title: Influence of Skin Oil Films on Ozonolysis Kinetics in Simulated Indoor Aqueous Aerosols and Surface Films
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that reactions of ozone with skin-sourced squalene on surfaces and in the gas phase significantly impact indoor air quality and personal exposure. However, the role of aqueous phases—like those in respiratory aerosols, surface water films, or sweat—in these processes is poorly understood. Organic surface films, ubiquitous indoors from human activities and product use, may critically alter the partitioning, reactivity, and products of aqueous ozonolysis, with direct implications for inhalation and dermal uptake of pollutants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a surface film of skin lipids (e.g., squalene, oleic acid) will modulate the kinetics and mechanism of ozone-driven reactions in an underlying aqueous phase by acting as a reactive sink, a diffusion barrier, and a source of secondary organic compounds that partition into the water.
proposals.method: We will employ a coated-wetted-wall flow reactor coupled to a proton-transfer-reaction mass spectrometer (PTR-MS) and an ozone analyzer. Aqueous solutions with controlled pH and ionic strength will be introduced as a thin film on a glass or Teflon cylinder. A reproducible surface film of synthetic skin oil components will be applied using a micro-syringe. We will measure the ozone uptake coefficient (γ) and product evolution (e.g., aldehydes, carboxylic acids) as a function of film thickness, ozone concentration, and relative humidity, comparing results to film-free aqueous controls and gas-surface ozonolysis experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, mechanistic data on how organic surface films alter multiphase ozone chemistry in indoor-relevant aqueous systems. It will refine models of indoor oxidant cycling and human exposure by incorporating a previously neglected but prevalent reactive interface. The findings will directly inform assessments of the health implications of reactions involving indoor ozone, human emissions, and water.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied gas-surface or bulk aqueous ozonolysis, the explicit experimental investigation of a *surface film's* influence on *underlying aqueous-phase* reaction kinetics represents a novel synthesis of Weschler's expertise in skin oil chemistry with emerging questions in indoor multiphase chemistry.

proposals.title: Influence of Organic Surface Films on the Scavenging Efficiency of Ozone and Ozonolysis Products by Indoor Aerosols and Droplets
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that skin oil lipids like squalene react with ozone at indoor surfaces, forming semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs) that partition to airborne particles and droplets. The scavenging of these gas-phase products by aerosols is a key process governing indoor air quality and human exposure. However, the role of organic films, which ubiquitously coat indoor surfaces and particles, in modulating this scavenging efficiency is poorly quantified, creating a critical gap in exposure models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the formation of organic films, derived from skin oil ozonolysis and other indoor sources, on the surface of aerosols and droplets reduces the mass accommodation coefficients and overall scavenging efficiency for key ozonolysis products (e.g., 6-MHO, 4-OPA) and ozone itself.
proposals.method: We will employ a controlled environmental chamber to generate and age surrogate indoor aerosols (e.g., ammonium sulfate, NaCl) in the presence of known skin oil ozonolysis products. Using a tandem differential mobility analyzer (TDMA) system, we will measure changes in particle hygroscopicity and volatility as a proxy for film formation. Aerosol scavenging efficiency will be quantified by exposing these film-coated particles to specific gas-phase ozonolysis products in a flow reactor and measuring the gas-phase depletion via Proton-Transfer-Reaction Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometry (PTR-ToF-MS). Parallel experiments will use a coated-wetted-wall flow reactor to measure the uptake of ozone and products onto water droplets with and without synthetic squalene ozonolysis films.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimentally determined scaling factors for how organic films reduce scavenging rates, a critical parameter for indoor chemistry models. It will directly improve the accuracy of predictions for human exposure to secondary pollutants derived from ozone-human chemistry. The results will inform the assessment of health risks and the efficacy of air cleaning strategies that alter indoor oxidant levels.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized film formation and gas-surface reactions, the specific impact of these films on the subsequent aerosol and droplet scavenging phase—a central removal pathway—has not been systematically measured. This bridges the gap between surface chemistry and aerosol dynamics in the human-occupied indoor environment.

proposals.title: A Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic (PBPK) Model for the Respiratory Tract Uptake and Fate of Ozone and Its Primary Reaction Products with Squalene
proposals.motivation: Indoor ozone reacts rapidly with skin-surface squalene, generating a complex mixture of carbonyls, acids, and other products. While the chemistry is well-characterized, the subsequent inhalation exposure and respiratory tract dosimetry of these co-produced species remain poorly quantified. This gap limits our ability to assess the integrated health risks from personal ozone exposure, particularly for reactive products formed directly in the breathing zone.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-compartment PBPK model, parameterized with known reaction kinetics and physicochemical properties, will predict significantly different regional respiratory tract deposition patterns and systemic uptake for ozone versus its key squalene oxidation products (e.g., 6-methyl-5-hepten-2-one, geranyl acetone).
proposals.method: We will adapt an existing multi-compartment PBPK framework for the respiratory tract, defining compartments for the extrathoracic, tracheobronchial, and alveolar regions. The model will integrate: 1) a chemical module simulating the ozone-squalene reaction rate and product yields based on established gas-phase kinetics and skin-oil composition; 2) a physiochemical module using the SPARC or EPI Suite to estimate partition coefficients and reaction rates for key products; and 3) a dosimetry module solving mass-balance equations to track species-specific absorption, exhalation, and mucociliary clearance. Model inputs will be informed by controlled human exposure chamber data on personal ozone removal rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework linking personal ozone chemistry to internal dose estimates for its reaction byproducts. It will identify which squalene ozonolysis products are likely to reach sensitive lung regions or enter systemic circulation. The model will serve as a critical tool for translating measured personal exposure metrics into biologically relevant doses for epidemiological and toxicological studies.
proposals.novelty: While PBPK models exist for inhaled ozone and some VOCs, none have been developed to simulate the simultaneous, dynamic inhalation of a primary oxidant and the complex mixture of secondary products generated *on the person* in real-time, a unique exposure scenario central to the 'human oxidation field' concept.

proposals.title: High-Resolution, Real-Time Mapping of Indoor Ozone Exposure Using Dense IoT Sensor Networks
proposals.motivation: Indoor ozone reacts with skin lipids like squalene to generate potentially harmful secondary pollutants, yet exposure is highly dynamic and spatially variable. Traditional single-point monitoring fails to capture this heterogeneity, limiting our understanding of personal exposure and its health implications. Deploying dense, low-cost sensor networks can address this gap by mapping ozone concentrations at the scale of human activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dense networks of low-cost ozone sensors will reveal significant, predictable spatiotemporal gradients in indoor ozone concentrations, driven by proximity to outdoor air sources, occupant density, and the distribution of reactive surfaces (e.g., skin, furnishings).
proposals.method: We will deploy a network of 30-50 calibrated, low-cost electrochemical ozone sensors (e.g., based on pre-2026 Alphasense O3-B4 cells) in a representative office or residential environment. Sensors will transmit data via Wi-Fi or LoRaWAN to a central hub. We will apply spatial interpolation algorithms (e.g., kriging) and real-time mapping software (e.g., Grafana) to create continuous 2D exposure maps. Concurrent measurements of occupancy, ventilation rate, and outdoor ozone will be used to validate and interpret the observed patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fine-grained, dynamic maps of indoor ozone, directly linking spatial concentration fields to potential human exposure. It will establish a methodological framework for using IoT networks in indoor air chemistry and offer empirical data to validate and refine existing models of indoor ozone dynamics and skin surface chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While sensor networks are used for outdoor pollution, their application for high-resolution indoor ozone mapping, specifically to visualize the 'exposure landscape' for reactive skin chemistry, is novel. This moves beyond bulk concentration measurements to a spatially explicit understanding of exposure drivers.

proposals.title: Modeling Indoor Oxidation Chemistry: Applying Chemical Reactor Theory to Predict Human Exposure to Ozone-Skin Oil Reaction Products
proposals.motivation: Indoor ozone reacts with skin lipids like squalene on surfaces and in the air, generating oxidation products (e.g., aldehydes, ketones) that can impact respiratory health and sensory perception. Current indoor air models often treat these reactions simplistically, lacking the formal kinetic framework to predict spatial and temporal concentration gradients of these products, especially near human occupants who are both sources and sinks.
proposals.hypothesis: Indoor spaces can be accurately modeled as networks of well-mixed (CSTR) and plug-flow (PFR) reactors, where the resulting product concentrations from ozone-skin oil chemistry are governed by reactor-specific residence times, mixing regimes, and surface-to-volume ratios.
proposals.method: We will adapt established chemical reactor models from chemical engineering. Specific algorithms will include solving coupled ordinary differential equations for mass balances in a well-mixed bedroom/living room model (CSTR) and the 1D advection-reaction equation for a hallway or ventilation duct (PFR). The kinetic core will integrate pre-2026 published rate constants and product yields for ozone-squalene reactions. The model will be parameterized and validated using existing experimental data from chamber studies measuring oxidation products under controlled ozone, temperature, and humidity conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, quantitative framework for predicting human exposure to secondary pollutants formed from indoor chemistry. It will directly link building ventilation parameters and geometry to personal exposure, offering a tool for assessing the impact of interventions like air cleaning or material selection. The model will advance the field from descriptive studies to predictive, mechanistic risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: While reactor theory is standard in engineering, its formal application to model the complex, multi-phase chemistry of the indoor environment—specifically treating the occupied zone as a distinct chemical reactor—is novel. This approach moves beyond simple box models by explicitly incorporating flow and mixing regimes that control reaction progress.

proposals.title: Source Apportionment of Indoor Secondary Organic Aerosols: Quantifying Contributions from Human Skin Oil Ozonolysis and Other Indoor Sources
proposals.motivation: Indoor air contains a complex mixture of oxidation products from reactions between ozone and various indoor-sourced volatile organic compounds (VOCs), including those from human skin oil (e.g., squalene). These secondary products, including carbonyls and organic aerosols, can impact indoor air quality and human health. However, the quantitative contribution of human-occupied-space chemistry versus other indoor sources (e.g., building materials, consumer products) to the overall burden of reactive species remains poorly resolved, limiting targeted exposure mitigation.
proposals.hypothesis: Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) applied to high-time-resolution measurements of indoor gas- and particle-phase oxidation products can resolve distinct source profiles, and the human-occupancy-derived factor will correlate dynamically with ozone concentration, occupancy, and specific squalene ozonolysis markers.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled chamber and real-room experiments measuring a comprehensive suite of VOCs, oxidized VOCs (e.g., 6-MHO, 4-OPA), and particle-phase organics with fast instrumentation (e.g., PTR-MS, aerosol mass spectrometer). The multivariate dataset will be analyzed using the US EPA PMF 5.0 model to statistically resolve source factors and their contributions. The Chemical Mass Balance (CMB) model will be used in parallel with source profiles from controlled ozonolysis of pure squalene and common indoor materials to provide a complementary, constraint-based apportionment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, source-resolved budget for indoor secondary organic aerosols and reactive gases, explicitly isolating the human oxidation field's contribution. It will establish a novel analytical framework for indoor air chemistry, moving beyond qualitative associations to mechanistic, quantifiable source impacts, directly informing building ventilation and filtration strategies.
proposals.novelty: While PMF and CMB are standard for outdoor air, their rigorous application to apportion *indoor* oxidation products to specific precursors (especially human skin oil) is novel. This adapts a core atmospheric science methodology to solve a central problem in indoor environmental chemistry, bridging two traditionally separate fields.

proposals.title: Direct Causal Links Between Ozone Exposure, Skin Oil Oxidation, and Acute Sensory Irritation: A Controlled Human Exposure Chamber Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that ozone reacts with skin lipids like squalene to generate a complex mixture of oxidation products (e.g., aldehydes, ketones) in the indoor environment. While these products are suspected of causing sensory irritation and other acute health effects, epidemiological and observational studies cannot disentangle them from co-occurring pollutants, leaving a critical gap in establishing direct, causal relationships for specific compounds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled, transient exposure of human volunteers to realistic indoor ozone levels (30-60 ppb) will generate a quantifiable, personal cloud of specific squalene oxidation products (e.g., 6-MHO, geranyl acetone) and that the concentration of these products in the breathing zone will be directly correlated with objective and subjective measures of sensory irritation.
proposals.method: We will conduct human challenge studies in a climate-controlled exposure chamber. Volunteers (n=20-30) will undergo sessions with and without pre-applied standardized skin oil (squalene). Real-time proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometry (PTR-TOF-MS) will monitor dynamic concentrations of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and oxidation products in the chamber and personal breathing zone. Concurrently, we will measure physiological markers (e.g., eye blink frequency, nasal airflow resistance) and collect subjective irritation reports via standardized questionnaires. Statistical analysis will employ mixed-effects models to link ozone dose, personal product generation, and irritation endpoints, controlling for humidity, temperature, and clothing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, causal evidence linking specific, person-generated oxidation products to acute physiological responses. It will quantify the 'personal reactive chemistry' dose, moving beyond bulk chamber measurements. The findings will critically inform indoor air quality guidelines and risk assessments for ozone by identifying the most biologically relevant secondary pollutants.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has modeled or measured product formation, this study uniquely applies the controlled-exposure clinical trial paradigm to indoor air chemistry, directly testing causality in humans. It integrates real-time, high-resolution personal chemical exposure monitoring with concurrent clinical irritation metrics, a novel synthesis of environmental chemistry and clinical toxicology methods.

proposals.title: A Wearable Sensor Platform for Breath-Resolved Personal Dosimetry of Ozone and Its Primary Skin-Oil Reaction Products
proposals.motivation: Indoor ozone reacts rapidly with human skin lipids like squalene, generating a personal cloud of oxidation products (e.g., aldehydes, ketones) that are subsequently inhaled. Pre-2026 research has characterized bulk room concentrations and surface reactions, but the actual personal intake dose, which varies with activity, proximity to sources, and individual physiology, remains unquantified. This gap limits accurate assessment of personal exposure and its potential health effects, particularly for sensitive populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a wearable monitor sampling air in the immediate breathing zone can measure breath-by-breath intake of ozone and its key squalene ozonolysis products (e.g., 6-MHO), and that this personal dose will be significantly higher and more variable than estimates derived from stationary indoor air measurements.
proposals.method: We will develop a chest-worn monitor integrating a miniaturized ozone sensor (e.g., metal-oxide semiconductor) and a selective adsorbent cartridge for carbonyl compounds, synchronized with a commercial respiration sensor (e.g., based on thoracic impedance). The system will use a microcontroller to time-resolve sampling to the inhalation phase of the breath cycle. A key algorithm will correlate sensor data with the respiratory flow rate to calculate per-breath and cumulative intake (dose = ∫ Concentration(t) * Flow Rate(t) dt). The system will be validated in controlled chamber studies against reference instruments before deployment in real-world settings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct measurements of personal, breath-resolved intake doses for ozone and its primary skin-oil reaction products. It will establish a methodological framework for personal inhalation dosimetry of reactive indoor pollutants. The resulting data will critically inform exposure science and risk assessment models by linking personal activity and physiology to chemical intake.
proposals.novelty: While personal ozone monitors exist, none are synchronized with breathing to provide true intake dosimetry. Furthermore, no wearable system has attempted concurrent, time-resolved measurement of ozone and its specific, rapidly formed personal cloud products, moving beyond bulk room measurements to the individual's true chemical exposure.

proposals.title: Characterizing the Composition and Capacity of Condensed-Phase SVOC Reservoirs on Common Indoor Surface Materials
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including seminal work by Weschler and others, has established that semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs) partition dynamically between indoor air, airborne particles, and surfaces, influencing human exposure and indoor chemistry. However, the capacity, composition, and material-specific properties of these condensed-phase surface reservoirs remain poorly quantified. This gap limits our ability to model SVOC dynamics, predict human exposure, and understand the role of surfaces in mediating ozone-skin oil and ozone-surface reactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the composition and mass loading of condensed-phase SVOC reservoirs vary systematically with surface material type (e.g., painted drywall, vinyl flooring, wood, glass, fabric) and are influenced by the history of indoor human occupancy and product use, reflecting the deposition and partitioning of skin lipids (e.g., squalene), personal care product constituents, and other indoor SVOCs.
proposals.method: We will sample SVOCs from a diverse set of pre-characterized indoor surface materials in occupied residential and office environments using solvent-impregnated wipes and surface film lifts. Samples will be analyzed via gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to quantify a targeted suite of SVOCs, including squalene oxidation products, phthalates, organophosphates, and siloxanes. Concurrent measurements of indoor air SVOC concentrations, ozone, and occupancy patterns will be collected. Statistical models (e.g., principal component analysis and linear mixed-effects models) will be used to correlate surface reservoir composition with material properties, environmental conditions, and human activity factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, material-specific inventory of condensed-phase SVOC reservoirs in real-world settings. It will yield crucial input parameters for indoor fate and exposure models, improving their predictive accuracy. Furthermore, it will elucidate the role of surfaces as both a sink and a potential secondary source of SVOCs and reactive intermediates from ozone-surface interactions.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have measured SVOCs on specific surfaces (e.g., windows), a systematic comparison across a wide range of common indoor materials, explicitly linked to human-associated compounds like skin lipids, has not been conducted. This study directly connects Weschler's established research on ozone-skin chemistry to the broader landscape of indoor surface reservoirs.

proposals.title: Aggregate Emissions of SVOCs from Indoor Reservoirs: A City-Scale Modeling Framework for Urban Housing Stocks
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that indoor surfaces, including skin, act as reservoirs for semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs), which can be re-emitted and transformed via ozone-initiated chemistry, impacting indoor air quality and occupant exposure. However, exposure assessments and urban air quality models largely ignore the aggregate contribution of these indoor reservoirs across a city's building stock. Quantifying this city-scale source is critical for understanding population-level exposure to SVOCs and their oxidation products, which have documented health implications.
proposals.hypothesis: The aggregate emissions of SVOCs from indoor reservoirs (skin, clothing, surfaces) across a metropolitan housing stock constitute a significant, previously unquantified source influencing urban-scale SVOC concentrations and population exposure when integrated with outdoor-indoor exchange.
proposals.method: We will develop a bottom-up stochastic model using a Monte Carlo framework. The model will integrate pre-2026 datasets: (1) housing stock characteristics (age, type, volume) from municipal/census data, (2) distributions of indoor environmental parameters (temperature, humidity, air exchange rates) from prior studies, (3) occupant density and activity patterns, and (4) experimentally derived SVOC (e.g., from squalene oxidation) emission factors from skin and surface reservoirs as functions of ozone concentration, temperature, and humidity (based on Weschler's prior work). The model will simulate emissions for thousands of representative dwellings, scaling results to the city level using building stock statistics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative estimate of city-scale SVOC emissions from indoor reservoirs, establishing a novel source term for urban air quality models. It will create a scalable framework to assess the population exposure implications of indoor chemistry and inform policies on building ventilation and urban ozone management by highlighting the indirect role of the indoor environment on outdoor SVOC burdens.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized SVOC dynamics in single-zone chambers or individual homes, this proposal scales the process to an entire urban housing stock, explicitly linking indoor reservoir chemistry to outdoor urban air quality modeling—a spatial and conceptual integration not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Size-Resolved Analysis of Ozone-Skin Oil Oxidation Products in Settled Dust: Implications for Inhalation and Dermal Exposure
proposals.motivation: Indoor ozone reacts with skin lipids like squalene on surfaces and in settled dust, generating bioactive carbonyls and organic aerosols. Pre-2026 research has established these reactions but treats settled dust as a bulk reservoir, ignoring particle size distribution. Since size dictates dust resuspension potential, inhalation deposition efficiency, and dermal adherence, a size-fractionated analysis is critical for accurate exposure assessment and understanding differential health risks from these ubiquitous pollutants.
proposals.hypothesis: The concentration and speciation of ozone-skin oil oxidation products (e.g., 6-methyl-5-hepten-2-one, geranyl acetone, other squalene ozonolysis products) in settled dust vary systematically with particle size, with finer fractions (<10 μm, PM10) containing a higher mass fraction of these semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs) and representing a greater potential source for human exposure via inhalation and dermal transfer.
proposals.method: Settled dust will be collected from environments with known human occupancy and characterized ozone levels. Samples will be dry-sieved and aerodynamically separated (e.g., using a cascade impactor) into defined size fractions (e.g., >100 μm, 10-100 μm, 2.5-10 μm, <2.5 μm). Each fraction will be solvent-extracted and analyzed via gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) for target squalene oxidation products. Concurrently, the oxidative potential of size fractions will be assessed using a dithiothreitol (DTT) assay. Statistical analysis (e.g., ANOVA, correlation) will link product concentrations to particle size, dust composition (via elemental analysis), and environmental parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative dataset on the size-dependent partitioning of skin-oil derived oxidation products in dust, directly informing models of inhalation and dermal exposure to these reactive compounds. It will establish whether fine dust acts as a concentrated reservoir for bioactive SVOCs, thereby refining risk assessments for indoor air quality and contributing to a mechanistic understanding of personal cloud chemistry and its health implications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Weschler and others has characterized bulk dust chemistry and gas-surface reactions, this proposal uniquely integrates particle size-resolved analysis with specific ozone-skin oil reaction product tracing to mechanistically link indoor chemistry to a primary exposure pathway (resuspended dust).

proposals.title: Bioaccessibility of Ozone-Derived Contaminants in Indoor Dust: Linking Surface Chemistry to Dermal Exposure
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including by Weschler, has established that ozone reacts with skin lipids like squalene on indoor surfaces and in settled dust, generating bioactive carbonyls and organic acids. While total concentrations of these reaction products in dust are measurable, their potential for human exposure via dermal contact remains unquantified. Assessing the bioaccessible fraction is critical for moving from environmental concentration to a meaningful estimate of internal dose and health risk, particularly for vulnerable populations like infants.
proposals.hypothesis: The bioaccessible fraction of ozone-derived carbonyl compounds (e.g., 6-methyl-5-hepten-2-one, geranyl acetone) and co-associated metals in indoor settled dust is modulated by dust composition (organic carbon content, pH) and is significantly lower than the total extracted concentration, but still represents a non-negligible dermal exposure pathway.
proposals.method: We will collect settled dust from diverse indoor environments (homes, offices) characterized for ozone and occupant density. A standardized in vitro physiologically based extraction test (PBET) for dermal bioaccessibility will be adapted, using synthetic sweat (pH 4.5-6.5) as the extraction fluid. Dust aliquots will be subjected to: 1) total solvent extraction (GC-MS/MS for target carbonyls, ICP-MS for metals), and 2) the PBET protocol. The bioaccessible fraction will be quantified as the ratio of PBET-extracted to total concentration. Statistical analysis (multiple linear regression) will relate bioaccessibility to dust properties (organic carbon, pH, specific surface area).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative data on the dermal bioaccessibility of key secondary organic contaminants formed via indoor ozone chemistry. It will establish a crucial scaling factor for translating measured dust concentrations into estimates of actual dermal dose. The findings will directly inform more accurate risk assessments and refine models of total personal exposure to indoor-generated pollutants.
proposals.novelty: While bioaccessibility studies for dust-borne metals and legacy pollutants exist, this approach is novel for dynamically formed, semi-volatile ozone-skin oil reaction products. It uniquely bridges Weschler's established research on indoor surface chemistry and the human oxidation field with the exposure science paradigm of bioavailability.

proposals.title: Quantifying Dermal Uptake of SVOCs: A Novel Framework Using Personal Air and Skin-Surface Concentrations as Primary Exposure Metrics
proposals.motivation: Traditional exposure assessment for semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs) relies heavily on bulk indoor air concentrations, which poorly represent the microenvironment at the human boundary layer where dermal uptake occurs. This is a critical gap, as dermal uptake is a major, often dominant, pathway for many SVOCs like phthalates and flame retardants. A more accurate, person-centric metric is needed to improve exposure science and health risk assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: For dermal uptake of SVOCs, the concentration in the personal air space immediately adjacent to the skin, and the resulting equilibrium concentration on the skin surface, are superior predictors of internal dose than bulk room air concentration.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled chamber studies with human volunteers, measuring SVOC concentrations (e.g., DEP, DnBP) simultaneously in bulk chamber air, personal air via lapel samplers, and directly on the skin surface using dermal patches. Concurrent measurements of ozone, temperature, humidity, and squalene oxidation products will characterize the personal oxidation field. A mechanistic mass-balance model will be developed, treating the skin surface and personal air as a coupled compartment, to predict skin-surface concentration from personal air concentration and key parameters (e.g., air velocity, skin lipid composition, clothing).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, mechanistic framework for using personal air and skin-surface concentrations as the primary exposure metric for dermal uptake of SVOCs. It will yield new partition coefficients and mass transfer coefficients for the personal microenvironment. The results will directly inform more accurate exposure models and refine risk assessments for a wide range of SVOCs.
proposals.novelty: While Weschler's prior work established the importance of the personal oxidation field for reactive species, this proposal uniquely applies the 'personal cloud' concept to the dermal uptake pathway for non-reactive SVOCs, explicitly linking personal air and skin-surface concentrations in a predictive model.

proposals.title: A Mechanistic Model for Dermal Uptake of Indoor Semivolatile Organic Compounds (SVOCs): Integrating Air-Skin Partitioning and Transdermal Transport
proposals.motivation: Human exposure to indoor SVOCs, such as phthalates and flame retardants, is a significant public health concern due to their endocrine-disrupting properties. While inhalation and ingestion pathways are studied, dermal uptake from air is poorly quantified, representing a critical knowledge gap in exposure assessment and risk evaluation. A predictive model is needed to understand how SVOC physicochemical properties, indoor environmental conditions, and skin state govern this transport process.
proposals.hypothesis: The dermal uptake flux of an SVOC from indoor air can be predicted by a two-resistance model that integrates (1) air-to-skin surface partitioning, governed by the SVOC's octanol-air partition coefficient (Koa) and skin surface lipid composition, and (2) transdermal transport, governed by its stratum corneum-water partition coefficient (Ksc/w) and diffusivity.
proposals.method: We will develop a mechanistic, steady-state model building on the fugacity-based framework for SVOC partitioning to clothing and skin oils established by Weschler and colleagues. The model will treat the skin as a two-layer membrane: a surface lipid film (e.g., squalene, triglycerides) and the underlying stratum corneum. Partitioning to the surface film will be modeled using Koa and the composition of skin oil. Transdermal transport will be modeled using Fickian diffusion, with input parameters (Ksc/w, diffusivity) derived from existing quantitative structure-property relationship (QSPR) algorithms for SVOCs. The model will be implemented in Python and validated against existing experimental dermal uptake data for compounds like diethyl phthalate and benzyl benzoate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, mechanistic model for predicting dermal uptake of SVOCs directly from air, moving beyond empirical correlations. It will yield a quantitative tool for exposure scientists to refine total exposure estimates and for epidemiologists to better attribute health outcomes. The model will identify the key chemical and environmental parameters controlling this pathway, guiding future experimental research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized SVOC partitioning to skin surfaces or transport from contaminated surfaces, no model has fully integrated air-to-skin partitioning with subsequent diffusion through the skin barrier for airborne SVOCs. This explicitly links Weschler's research on the skin's surface chemistry (e.g., ozone reactions with squalene) to the internal dose from gaseous pollutants.

proposals.title: Non-Volatile and Particle-Phase Products from Ozone-Skin Oil Chemistry: A Reservoir for Prolonged Dermal and Inhalation Exposure
proposals.motivation: Extensive pre-2026 research, including by Weschler and colleagues, has characterized the formation of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from ozone reactions with skin lipids like squalene, focusing on their role in indoor air chemistry and inhalation exposure. However, the assumption that reaction products are primarily volatile overlooks the potential for low-volatility organic compounds (LVOCs) and secondary organic aerosols (SOA) to form and persist on skin and clothing surfaces. This creates a significant knowledge gap regarding a reservoir of chemicals that could lead to prolonged dermal uptake or be resuspended for inhalation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ozone-initiated oxidation of skin surface lipids generates a significant fraction of non-volatile and particle-phase reaction products that remain sequestered on skin and clothing fabrics, acting as a sustained source for dermal and inhalation exposure.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled chamber experiments with human participants and fabric swatches (cotton, polyester) exposed to realistic indoor ozone levels (~20-40 ppb). Skin surface and fabric samples will be analyzed pre- and post-exposure using a multi-platform analytical approach. Non-volatile residues will be solvent-extracted and characterized via liquid chromatography coupled to high-resolution mass spectrometry (LC-HRMS) for molecular identification. Particle-phase products collected on filters and via electrostatic precipitation will be analyzed for size distribution (SMPS) and chemical composition using thermal desorption–gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (TD-GC-MS) and aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS). A key algorithm will involve non-targeted analysis of LC-HRMS data to identify oxidation products with estimated vapor pressures below 10^-4 Pa.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence and chemical characterization of the non-volatile reservoir resulting from indoor ozone-human chemistry. It will quantify the partitioning of products between gas, particle, and surface phases, fundamentally expanding exposure models beyond VOCs. The findings will inform risk assessments concerning dermal absorption of oxidation products and the role of clothing as a sink and source of indoor pollutants.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant paradigm in indoor chemistry that focuses on volatile emissions, instead targeting the persistent, non-volatile fraction. It uniquely integrates surface chemistry on skin and textiles with aerosol formation, bridging personal exposure and indoor air quality in a novel way.

proposals.title: Beyond the Skin Surface: Shed Skin Flakes and Clothing Fibers as Persistent, Mobile Reaction Sites for Indoor Ozonolysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established the skin surface as a primary reaction zone for ozone, generating volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and oxidation products that impact indoor air quality and personal exposure. However, this paradigm assumes reactions are confined to the living skin-air interface. Shed skin flakes (squamae) and clothing fibers, which are ubiquitous in indoor environments and rich in ozone-reactive squalene and other skin lipids, may constitute a previously overlooked, persistent reservoir for ongoing ozonolysis, extending the spatial and temporal scale of this chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that shed human skin flakes and clothing fibers act as mobile, long-lived reaction sites for ozone, continuing to generate VOC products (e.g., 6-MHO, 4-OPA) and secondary organic aerosols long after detachment from the human body, thereby contributing significantly to the background oxidative and chemical burden in occupied indoor spaces.
proposals.method: We will collect shed skin flakes and worn cotton fibers from volunteers under controlled conditions. These substrates will be exposed to realistic indoor ozone concentrations (20-50 ppb) in a small, well-characterized Teflon chamber. Using Proton-Transfer-Reaction Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometry (PTR-ToF-MS), we will quantify the real-time production of key squalene ozonolysis products. Complementary offline analysis via Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS) will identify non-volatile reaction products. A kinetic multilayer model of surface and particle chemistry (KM-SUB) will be adapted to simulate the reaction dynamics on these porous, fragmented substrates compared to intact skin.
proposals.contribution: This work will expand the conceptual model of indoor human chemistry by identifying a decoupled, persistent source of reactive chemistry. It will provide the first quantitative data on VOC emission rates from shed biological material, informing indoor air quality models. The findings could also explain a portion of the background SOA and VOC concentrations in indoor environments not directly linked to immediate human presence.
proposals.novelty: While the reactivity of skin surface lipids is known, the ongoing chemical activity of *detached* skin material and its carrier fabrics has not been systematically investigated. This shifts the focus from a personal, immediate 'oxidation field' to a distributed, residual reaction network in the indoor environment.

proposals.title: Real-Time Breath Analysis of Ozone-Skin Oil Reaction Products: Quantifying Personal Uptake and Metabolic Response
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that ozone reacts with squalene and other skin-oil constituents on surfaces and skin, generating a complex mixture of carbonyls and other volatile organic compounds (VOCs). While chamber studies have measured emissions, the personal, internal dose of these reaction products and their subsequent human metabolism remain poorly characterized. This gap limits our understanding of the true health impacts of indoor ozone chemistry on building occupants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, ozone-initiated reaction products from skin oil (e.g., 6-methyl-5-hepten-2-one, geranyl acetone) and their potential metabolites can be detected in exhaled breath in real-time, and that their breath concentrations will correlate dynamically with personal ozone exposure and activity patterns.
proposals.method: We will employ proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometry (PTR-ToF-MS) for high-time-resolution (sub-minute) analysis of exhaled breath from human volunteers in controlled environmental chambers with precisely modulated ozone levels. Participants will undergo protocols involving varying ozone concentrations, activity levels, and clothing scenarios. Breath data will be synchronized with real-time ozone, VOC, and environmental measurements. Multivariate statistical analysis, including principal component analysis (PCA) and time-series correlation algorithms, will be used to link breath signatures to exposure conditions and identify potential metabolic transformation products.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, real-time evidence of personal uptake and potential metabolism of ozone-initiated skin-oil oxidation products. It will establish a novel methodology for linking dynamic environmental exposures to internal biological markers, moving beyond static chamber emission measurements. The findings will critically inform exposure science and risk assessment related to indoor air chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While Weschler's prior work modeled surface reactions and measured room-air concentrations, this proposal directly targets the *personal internal dose* using real-time breath analysis, a non-invasive biomarker approach not previously applied to this specific chemical pathway. It transforms the study of indoor ozone chemistry from an environmental measurement problem to a personalized exposure and pharmacokinetic question.

proposals.title: Development and Evaluation of Topical Ozone Scavengers to Reduce Secondary Organic Aerosol Formation from Human Skin Ozonolysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone reacts with skin lipids like squalene, generating volatile organic compounds (VOCs) that form secondary organic aerosols (SOA) and other oxidation products in the indoor environment. These reaction products can impact indoor air quality and potentially human health. Current work primarily measures these products; a proactive mitigation strategy is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Topically applied formulations containing specific, skin-compatible ozone scavengers (e.g., selected terpenes, ascorbic acid derivatives, or unsaturated fatty acids) will effectively reduce the flux of ozone to the skin surface, thereby suppressing the formation of squalene ozonolysis products (VOCs and SOA) in a controlled environmental chamber.
proposals.method: We will formulate simple, inert lotion bases with candidate scavengers. Using a controlled environmental chamber with human volunteers, we will measure real-time ozone decay and product formation (using PTR-MS and SMPS) for treated vs. untreated skin under standardized conditions (e.g., 30 ppb ozone, 50% RH). Key metrics will include the ozone deposition velocity to the skin, the yield of key VOCs (e.g., 6-MHO), and the resultant SOA mass concentration. A kinetic model will be used to quantify scavenging efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental proof-of-concept for a direct, personal intervention to mitigate an important indoor air chemistry source. It will establish a framework for evaluating the efficacy of topical scavengers and identify promising candidate compounds, shifting the field from observation to intervention.
proposals.novelty: While ozone removal by surfaces and air filters is known, the targeted application of reactive agents to the human skin specifically to prevent a well-characterized chemical pathway leading to indoor aerosols is a novel and untested mitigation paradigm.

proposals.title: Catalytic Surfaces in the Indoor Environment: Quantifying the Role of Common Materials in the Ozonolysis of Skin Oil Constituents
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 indoor chemistry models often treat surfaces as passive sinks or static reservoirs, yet emerging evidence suggests surface-mediated reactions are significant. This oversight limits our understanding of indoor oxidant budgets and the formation of secondary pollutants, such as carbonyls and organic acids, which impact indoor air quality and human health.
proposals.hypothesis: Common indoor surface materials (e.g., painted drywall, glass, stainless steel) are not inert but act as catalysts, altering the rate and product distribution of ozone reactions with skin oil lipids like squalene compared to gas-phase or skin-surface reactions.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled laboratory experiments using a stirred-flow reactor system. Squalene and other key skin oil components will be applied in thin films to material coupons. Exposing these to ozone under realistic indoor conditions (5-50 ppb O3, 20-50% RH), we will measure reaction kinetics via proton-transfer-reaction mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) for volatile products and extract surface films for detailed analysis via gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to identify non-volatile products. Experiments will compare reaction rates and product yields across materials and against a Teflon-coated inert control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, material-specific catalytic rate constants for a key indoor ozone sink, directly challenging the inert sink paradigm. It will improve predictive models of indoor air chemistry by incorporating surface-specific reactivity parameters and inform material selection for healthier indoor environments.
proposals.novelty: While Weschler's prior work established the skin surface as a reactive interface, this proposal systematically shifts focus to ubiquitous *inanimate* surfaces, treating them as active, variable participants in chemical cycles rather than passive backdrops.

proposals.title: Direct, Real-Time Measurement of Labile Squalene Ozonolysis Products at the Skin Surface: A Comparison of Thermal Desorption Proton-Transfer-Reaction Mass Spectrometry (TD-PTR-MS) with Conventional GC/MS
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone reactions with skin lipids like squalene generate a complex mixture of oxidation products, some of which are irritants or precursors to secondary aerosols. Standard analytical methods, such as solvent extraction followed by GC/MS, may miss highly reactive, low-volatility, or thermally labile compounds formed in these reactions, leading to an incomplete understanding of personal exposure and indoor air chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specialized, real-time thermal desorption techniques coupled with soft ionization mass spectrometry will capture a significantly broader and more representative suite of labile squalene ozonolysis products at the skin-air interface compared to conventional GC/MS methods, revealing previously overlooked compounds with potential health relevance.
proposals.method: We will employ a controlled human exposure chamber study where participants' forearms are exposed to realistic indoor ozone levels (~30 ppb). Emissions from the skin surface will be sampled in parallel using two methods: 1) Conventional method: collection on sorbent tubes followed by solvent extraction and GC/MS analysis. 2) Novel method: direct, real-time sampling using a thermal desorption unit (e.g., MARKES International) interfaced with a Proton-Transfer-Reaction Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometer (PTR-TOF-MS). The TD-PTR-MS method will use a low-temperature desorption ramp (30-150°C) to transfer compounds without thermal decomposition, with PTR-MS providing soft ionization to detect labile species. Data analysis will involve non-targeted analysis of PTR-MS spectra and comparative quantification of key markers (e.g., 6-MHO, geranyl acetone) across both platforms.
proposals.contribution: This work will critically evaluate the analytical bias inherent in common methods for studying skin chemistry, providing a more complete product distribution from a key indoor reaction. It will deliver a validated, real-time method for measuring personal exposure to reactive organics and identify new candidate compounds for future toxicological study, thereby refining models of indoor air quality and personal oxidative stress.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of squalene-ozone chemistry is known, directly comparing the analytical yield of real-time, minimally invasive thermal desorption/soft ionization against standard GC/MS for *in vivo* human skin emissions is novel. This approach tests the specific assumption that conventional methods miss critical, labile products.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Oxidative Stress Potential of Indoor-Generated Organic Hydroperoxides from Ozone-Skin Oil Chemistry
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Weschler's own work, has established that ozone reactions with skin lipids like squalene generate a complex mixture of labile, multifunctional species, including organic hydroperoxides (ROOHs), which partition to indoor particles. While these species are known to be reactive and potentially toxic, their specific contribution to the oxidative stress and inflammatory potential of inhaled indoor particulate matter remains unquantified, representing a critical gap in understanding real-world indoor air health impacts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that organic hydroperoxides formed via ozone-squalene chemistry constitute a significant, quantifiable fraction of the oxidative potential (OP) of indoor fine particles, and that their concentration and OP are predictable functions of ozone level, occupant density, and air exchange rate.
proposals.method: The study will combine controlled environmental chamber experiments, analytical chemistry, and cellular assays. First, we will generate particles laden with ROOHs by exposing squalene-coated surfaces or human volunteers to realistic indoor ozone levels (20-60 ppb) in a climate-controlled chamber, collecting particles on filters. ROOHs will be quantified using a derivatization method with triphenylphosphine followed by HPLC-MS. Concurrently, the oxidative potential of these particles will be measured using the dithiothreitol (DTT) assay and an alveolar macrophage-based assay for reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. A multiple linear regression model will be developed to relate ROOH concentration and OP to key independent variables (ozone, humidity, occupancy).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, mechanistic link between a specific indoor chemistry pathway (ozone-skin oil) and a quantifiable health-relevant aerosol property (oxidative potential). It will yield a predictive model for estimating this health impact based on measurable indoor parameters, advancing indoor exposure science beyond bulk PM2.5 mass toward more toxicologically relevant metrics.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have identified the products of ozone-skin oil reactions, none have systematically isolated and quantified the contribution of the resulting labile hydroperoxides to the aerosol's oxidative burden, bridging detailed chemistry with direct toxicological assessment.

proposals.title: Surface Reactivity of Common Indoor Materials: Characterizing Ozone and Skin Oil Oxidation Sites on HVAC Components, Paint, and Carpets
proposals.motivation: Indoor surfaces are vast, chemically active reservoirs that mediate the fate of ozone and skin oil constituents like squalene, influencing indoor air quality and human exposure to oxidation products. Prior work by Weschler and others has focused on gas-phase chemistry and skin surface reactions, but the fundamental surface science of the materials themselves remains poorly characterized. Understanding the reactive sites on common indoor materials is critical for predicting and controlling the formation of secondary pollutants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the surface composition and functional groups of common indoor materials (e.g., metals in HVAC, polymers in carpets, pigments in paint) contain distinct, quantifiable reactive sites that govern the heterogeneous ozonolysis of deposited skin oil and the direct uptake of ozone.
proposals.method: We will apply X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and Attenuated Total Reflectance Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) to characterize pristine and conditioned samples of aluminum HVAC fins, latex wall paint, and nylon carpet fibers. Samples will be conditioned by controlled exposure to ozone and/or synthetic skin oil (squalene/oleic acid mixtures) in an environmental chamber. XPS will quantify elemental surface composition and oxidation states, while ATR-FTIR will identify specific organic functional groups (e.g., C=O, -OH, C-O-C) formed or consumed during reactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, molecular-scale characterization of reactive sites on key indoor materials, moving beyond bulk sorption models. It will establish a foundational dataset linking material composition to surface reactivity, enabling more accurate predictions of indoor air chemistry and informing the selection of low-reactive materials for healthier buildings.
proposals.novelty: While surface techniques are standard in materials science, their systematic application to characterize the intrinsic reactivity of common, complex indoor materials toward ozone/skin oil chemistry is novel. This bridges the gap between indoor air chemistry field studies and fundamental surface science.
