proposals.title: Toxicogenomic Profiling of Mouse Skin and Respiratory Tissues to Elucidate Mechanistic Pathways of Irritation from Ozone-Squalene Reaction Products
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone reacts with skin-surface squalene to generate a complex mixture of oxidation products (e.g., aldehydes, ketones, organic acids) that can cause sensory irritation and inflammation. However, the specific biological pathways and molecular initiating events triggered by this complex, dynamic mixture remain poorly characterized, limiting our ability to assess health risks and develop effective mitigation strategies for indoor environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to ozone-squalene reaction products will induce a distinct, dose-dependent toxicogenomic signature in mouse skin and respiratory tissues, revealing activation of specific pathways related to oxidative stress, inflammation, and skin barrier dysfunction that are not fully captured by traditional toxicological endpoints.
proposals.method: Following a standard 28-day inhalation bioassay in mice (C57BL/6) exposed to realistic concentrations of ozone-squalene mixtures in a controlled chamber, we will perform RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) on harvested skin (dorsal) and lung tissues. Differential gene expression analysis will be conducted using the DESeq2 algorithm. Pathway and network analysis will be performed using Ingenuity Pathway Analysis (IPA) and Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) to identify significantly perturbed biological pathways (e.g., Nrf2-mediated oxidative stress response, IL-17 signaling, epidermal development). Results will be correlated with histopathological scores and inflammatory cytokine levels from the same bioassay.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, genome-wide map of biological responses to a key indoor-derived mixture, moving beyond concentration measurements to understand pathway-level disruption. It will identify potential biomarkers of effect for indoor air quality studies and inform the biological plausibility of epidemiological links between ozone-driven chemistry and occupant symptoms.
proposals.novelty: While Weschler's prior work has masterfully characterized the chemistry and formation of these products, applying toxicogenomics to this specific indoor chemistry problem is novel. It integrates cutting-edge molecular profiling (pre-2026) with established indoor air chemistry to decode the biological impact of a complex, real-world mixture rather than a single compound.

proposals.title: Whole-Building as a Chemical Reactor: Real-Time Mapping of Ozone-Skin Oil Reaction Dynamics and Secondary Emissions
proposals.motivation: Indoor ozone reacts with human skin lipids like squalene, generating a complex mixture of volatile and semi-volatile organic compounds (VOCs and SVOCs) that impact indoor air quality and occupant health. Current understanding is based on chamber studies or localized measurements, failing to capture the integrated, dynamic nature of these processes as occupants move through and interact with a building's entire air volume. A holistic, real-time assessment is needed to quantify building-scale source strengths and the spatial-temporal evolution of reaction products.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a building's entire occupied air volume functions as a well-mixed flow reactor for ozone-skin oil chemistry, and that real-time, multi-point mapping of key reactants and products will reveal predictable spatial gradients and emission fluxes that scale with human occupancy and activity.
proposals.method: We will instrument a university office building with a spatially distributed network of real-time sensors for ozone, VOCs (e.g., via PTR-TOF-MS), and particles, synchronized with occupancy tracking via Wi-Fi/CO2. Key zones (e.g., densely occupied offices, circulation areas) will host inlet systems for detailed VOC speciation. We will develop and apply a Lagrangian reactive transport model, treating the building HVAC system as the reactor's flow control, to back-calculate whole-building source and sink rates for ozone and key reaction products (e.g., 6-MHO) from the spatial concentration fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical dataset of building-scale, human-driven oxidation chemistry, quantifying the total emission flux of secondary pollutants from ozone-squalene reactions. It will deliver a novel modeling framework for predicting indoor pollutant dynamics based on occupancy and HVAC operation, moving beyond static chamber-based source factors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized the chemistry on individual surfaces or in small chambers, this study is novel in treating the entire building as the experimental unit and applying reactive transport modeling to map real-time, spatially resolved chemistry driven by human occupancy at an unprecedented scale.

proposals.title: Evolution of Indoor Chemical Emissions and Reactivity: A Multi-Year Study of Material Aging and Cleaning Effects on Ozone-Surface Chemistry
proposals.motivation: Indoor chemistry models rely on emission and reactivity parameters derived from new materials, but real-world exposures occur over product lifespans of years. Pre-2026 work by Weschler and others has established the importance of skin lipids and consumer products as reactive surfaces and sources for ozone-initiated chemistry, yet how these parameters change with material aging, use, and cleaning cycles is unknown, leading to potentially large errors in long-term exposure assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The emission profiles of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from common indoor materials and the surface reactivity of skin oil residues will systematically evolve over time due to chemical aging and cleaning practices, significantly altering the predicted concentrations of oxidation products like aldehydes and organic aerosols.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled longitudinal study using a well-established environmental chamber system. Samples of common materials (e.g., vinyl flooring, painted drywall, fabric) and glass substrates coated with synthetic skin oil (squalene, oleic acid) will be subjected to accelerated aging (controlled ozone/T/UV exposure) and periodic, realistic cleaning protocols over a simulated 2-year period. VOC emissions will be measured via PTR-MS and GC-MS after defined intervals. Surface reactivity will be quantified by measuring ozone deposition velocities and product yields. A kinetics-based chemical model, building on the INDCM framework, will be parameterized with the time-series data to create a lifespan-dependent emission and reactivity module.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dataset and modeling framework for time-dependent emission factors and surface reactivities for key indoor materials. It will directly improve the accuracy of long-term indoor air quality and personal exposure models, particularly for secondary pollutants formed via ozone chemistry. The results will inform more sustainable product design and maintenance practices.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have characterized initial material properties, this is the first to explicitly track and model the *temporal scaling* of chemical emissions and surface reactivity over a product's usable lifespan, integrating the effects of both chemical aging and physical cleaning.

proposals.title: Kinetic Control of Squalene and Its Oxidation Byproducts at the Skin-Air Interface: Implications for Indoor Exposure
proposals.motivation: Indoor models for semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs) often assume equilibrium partitioning between air and surfaces, including skin. However, the skin surface is a dynamic, chemically reactive environment where ozone-initiated oxidation of squalene generates a cascade of lower-volatility products. Pre-2026 work by Weschler and others shows this chemistry is influenced by personal factors, suggesting that fast reaction kinetics and mass transfer limitations may dominate over equilibrium, fundamentally altering predicted personal exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the air-surface exchange of squalene and its major ozonolysis products (e.g., 6-MHO, geranyl acetone) at the skin interface is kinetically controlled by reaction rates and transport resistances, not by equilibrium partitioning, under typical indoor conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled human exposure chamber studies, varying ozone concentration, air velocity, relative humidity, and skin temperature. Real-time, spatially resolved measurements of squalene and its oxidation products in the immediate boundary layer above the skin (using SIFT-MS or PTR-MS) will be coupled with surface sampling via tape-stripping. A dual-resistance mass transfer model, incorporating a reactive boundary layer and surface reaction kinetics derived from prior work, will be developed. Model predictions assuming kinetic control will be statistically compared to those from an equilibrium partitioning model using the experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence for kinetic limitation in SVOC exchange at a highly relevant, reactive indoor surface. It will yield a quantitative framework to refine personal exposure models for reactive SVOCs, moving beyond equilibrium assumptions. The results will directly impact risk assessments for indoor air quality and the understanding of the 'personal cloud.'
proposals.novelty: While kinetic limitations are acknowledged for SVOCs on impervious surfaces, applying this concept to the chemically complex and variable skin-oil film, and testing it against real-time human emissions, is a novel and critical step. It challenges the foundational equilibrium assumption in a high-priority exposure scenario.

proposals.title: Dynamic Validation of the Equilibrium Partitioning Assumption for Ozone-Skin Oil Reactions at the Indoor Air-Surface Interface
proposals.motivation: Models of indoor air chemistry often assume instantaneous equilibrium partitioning of reactive species between air and surfaces, a simplification critical for predicting concentrations of oxidation products. However, the validity of this assumption under real-world, transient conditions—with fluctuating ozone, varying skin oil composition, and dynamic human activity—remains untested. This gap limits the accuracy of exposure assessments for compounds generated at the skin surface, such as squalene oxidation products.
proposals.hypothesis: The equilibrium partitioning assumption for ozone and skin oil constituents (e.g., squalene) at the air-skin surface interface is invalid under dynamic indoor conditions characterized by rapid changes in ozone concentration, air movement, and skin surface state.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled chamber experiments with human participants and surrogate skin surfaces (e.g., squalene-coated glass). Using fast-response ozone monitors (e.g., UV photometry) and proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometry (PTR-ToF-MS) for volatile products, we will measure time-resolved concentration gradients near the surface during induced ozone steps (0-50 ppb) and simulated activities. A two-resistance kinetic mass transfer model will be developed and fitted to the experimental data to quantify characteristic times for partitioning versus reaction, explicitly testing the equilibrium assumption.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental dataset to validate or refute a foundational modeling assumption in indoor chemistry. It will yield a quantitative framework for when equilibrium can be assumed and when kinetic limitations dominate, directly improving the predictive power of indoor air quality and human exposure models for reactive species.
proposals.novelty: While equilibrium is commonly assumed, no study has directly measured the kinetics of ozone uptake and product release from real skin under dynamic, indoor-relevant conditions. This bridges the gap between controlled fundamental studies and the complex, transient reality of occupied indoor environments.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Influence of Ventilation on the Partitioning Dynamics of Ozone-Derived Skin Oil SVOCs Using Controlled Tracer Gas Releases
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone-initiated chemistry on human skin oil generates semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs) that partition between air, surfaces, and airborne particles, influencing indoor air quality and personal exposure. However, the role of ventilation in controlling the fate and indoor concentrations of these specific, chemically generated SVOCs remains poorly quantified. Understanding this relationship is critical for predicting occupant exposure and for designing ventilation strategies that effectively mitigate secondary pollutants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that air exchange rate, as precisely measured by a tracer gas decay method, will be the dominant factor controlling the steady-state indoor air concentration and phase distribution of key squalene ozonolysis products (e.g., 6-methyl-5-hepten-2-one, geranyl acetone) under real-world indoor conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled chamber experiments simulating an occupied indoor space. A constant, low-level ozone source and a human surrogate (heated manikin coated with synthetic skin oil containing deuterated squalene as a chemical tracer) will generate SVOCs. Simultaneously, we will release a perfluorocarbon tracer (PFT) gas (e.g., perfluoromethylcyclohexane) as a conservative measure of air exchange. Using proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometry (PTR-TOF-MS) and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), we will measure the dynamic concentrations of both the tracer gas and the target SVOCs. The air exchange rate will be calculated from the exponential decay constant of the PFT. We will then apply a mass-balance model to correlate SVOC partitioning coefficients (Koa) and indoor concentrations with the directly measured ventilation rate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, mechanistic link between a fundamental indoor parameter (air exchange rate) and the indoor fate of a chemically important class of SVOCs. It will yield quantitative transfer functions that can be incorporated into indoor air quality models to better predict exposures to ozone-initiated skin oil pollutants. The results will inform building science and public health guidelines on the efficacy of ventilation for controlling reactive chemistry byproducts.
proposals.novelty: While tracer gases are standard for measuring ventilation, their concurrent use to deconvolve the dynamic partitioning of *non-conservative*, chemically generated SVOCs from a defined source (skin oil) in real time is a novel application. This approach moves beyond correlative studies to establish causative, quantitative relationships.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Dynamic Partitioning of Ozone-Reactive Skin Oil Components (Squalene and Its Oxidation Products) Between Human Skin, Clothing, and Indoor Air Using a Controlled Environmental Chamber
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone-initiated reactions with skin surface lipids, particularly squalene, are a major source of volatile and semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs) in occupied indoor environments. However, the dynamic partitioning of these reaction products between the skin surface, clothing layers, and the gas phase is poorly quantified, creating a critical gap in models predicting indoor air quality and personal chemical exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that clothing acts as a dynamic reservoir for squalene and its ozone oxidation products, significantly modulating their release into indoor air and re-exposure to skin, with partitioning coefficients dependent on specific compound volatility, fabric type, and environmental conditions (e.g., temperature, humidity).
proposals.method: We will employ a state-of-the-art, climate-controlled Teflon environmental chamber. A heated, sweat-emitting manikin section clad in fabrics (cotton, polyester) coated with a known amount of synthetic skin oil (squalene, fatty acids) will be exposed to controlled, low-level ozone (~30 ppb). Real-time proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometry (PTR-TOF-MS) will monitor gas-phase products. Thermal desorption tubes coupled with GC-MS will sample compounds adsorbed to fabric swatches at timed intervals. Partitioning coefficients and kinetics will be derived by modeling the time-resolved mass transfer between the oil-coated surface, fabric, and chamber air using a coupled kinetic model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimentally determined, compound-specific partitioning data for key skin-oil-derived SVOCs between skin, clothing, and air. It will directly improve mechanistic models of the 'human oxidation field' and personal cloud, leading to more accurate predictions of inhalation and dermal exposure to secondary pollutants in real-world indoor settings.
proposals.novelty: While chamber studies of skin oil-ozone reactions exist, none have systematically isolated and quantified the role of clothing as a mediating reservoir in this partitioning process. This study integrates controlled atmospheric chemistry methods with material science to address a key, unresolved pathway in human exposure science.

proposals.title: A Mechanistic Model for Estimating Population-Level Exposure to Indoor SVOCs: Integrating Human Oxidation Field Dynamics with Urban Housing Stock Data
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs) from building materials and consumer products are ubiquitous indoors and pose significant health risks. However, exposure assessments remain limited to single compounds in specific microenvironments, lacking a framework to scale mechanistic chemistry to population-level exposure. This gap hinders the ability of urban planners and public health officials to prioritize interventions for the most impactful SVOC sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a city-wide inventory of indoor SVOC exposure can be constructed by integrating mechanistic models of indoor air chemistry—specifically, ozone-initiated reactions with skin lipids (e.g., squalene) and surfaces—with demographic, housing stock, and urban meteorological data, revealing significant spatial and socioeconomic disparities in exposure.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic, agent-based model in Python. The core algorithm will adapt existing single-zone mass-balance models for SVOC emission, partitioning, and reactive chemistry (ozonolysis of skin oil) to represent a synthetic population of individuals in diverse housing types. Model parameters (e.g., air exchange rates, indoor ozone concentrations, occupant density) will be stochastically assigned based on pre-2026 datasets (e.g., RECS, AHHS) for a prototypical U.S. city. The model will simulate the dynamic formation and concentration of key SVOC reaction products (e.g., 6-MHO) across the urban housing stock, estimating population exposure distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first scalable, mechanistic framework for estimating aggregate urban exposure to SVOCs and their reaction products, moving beyond static inventories of primary emissions. It will identify key leverage points (e.g., building ventilation, occupant behavior) that drive population-level exposure variance, providing a novel tool for comparative risk assessment and targeted policy development.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Weschler and others has characterized the fundamental chemistry of the human oxidation field, applying this mechanistic understanding to model city-scale exposure by explicitly linking it to real-world housing and demographic variability is a novel and necessary advancement in indoor air science.

proposals.title: Beyond the Pump: Quantifying Ozone-Driven Emissions and Dermal Uptake of Terpenes and Skin Oil Oxidation Products from Personal Care Products During Storage and Disposal
proposals.motivation: Current exposure assessments for personal care products (PCPs) focus overwhelmingly on the active use phase. However, pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone can react with terpenes from products and skin lipids like squalene, generating potentially harmful secondary organic aerosols (SOAs) and carbonyls. The storage of partially used products in bathrooms and the disposal of product residues in waste bins represent prolonged, overlooked periods where these reactive ingredients are exposed to indoor ozone, creating a persistent, low-level source of pollutants that may contribute to chronic indoor air quality issues and dermal exposure via contact with contaminated surfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the integrated mass of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and oxidation products emitted from PCPs during long-term storage and post-disposal periods is significant compared to emissions during brief active use, and that dermal uptake of these compounds from contact with product packaging and waste surfaces constitutes a meaningful exposure pathway.
proposals.method: The study will employ a combination of controlled chamber experiments and modeling. First, we will measure real-time VOC (e.g., limonene, linalool) and particle emissions from common PCPs (lotions, shampoos) in Teflon chambers under realistic indoor ozone concentrations (20-40 ppb) over extended periods (weeks) simulating storage. Second, we will use a validated, pre-2026 kinetic model (e.g., based on the GECKO-A generator) to simulate the multi-phase chemistry of ozone with product terpenes and squalene residues on packaging and in waste. Dermal uptake will be estimated using existing permeability coefficients for key oxidation products (e.g., 4-oxopentanal) and measured surface concentrations on packaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for assessing 'non-use' phase exposures from PCPs, shifting the paradigm in consumer product exposure assessment. It will yield emission factors for storage/disposal scenarios to improve indoor air quality models and identify specific oxidation products of concern for dermal uptake, informing more comprehensive risk evaluations.
proposals.novelty: While the chemistry of ozone with skin oil and VOCs is established, applying this mechanistic understanding to the prolonged, passive life-cycle stages of PCPs—where product matrices and residues alter reaction dynamics—is novel. It connects indoor chemistry directly to exposure science for product stages previously considered inert.

proposals.title: Identifying Keystone Stressors in Indoor VOC Mixtures: A Network Perturbation Approach to Risk Assessment
proposals.motivation: Indoor air contains complex mixtures of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from sources like building materials, consumer products, and human activities. Current risk assessment often focuses on individual, high-abundance compounds like formaldehyde, potentially overlooking synergistic or disproportionate effects from other mixture components. This gap mirrors historical ecological oversights where focusing solely on top predators missed critical system dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, non-formaldehyde VOCs or their synergistic combinations act as 'keystone stressors' in indoor emission mixtures, driving a disproportionate share of adverse biological responses that are not predictable from single-compound toxicity data.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled perturbation experiments in environmental chambers, systematically varying mixtures of VOCs commonly co-emitted indoors (e.g., terpenes, aldehydes, aromatics) with and without formaldehyde. Biological responses will be assessed using in vitro models of human lung epithelial cells, measuring endpoints like oxidative stress, cytokine release, and cytotoxicity. Response data will be analyzed using Gaussian Graphical Models and partial correlation networks to infer interaction networks and identify chemical nodes whose perturbation causes the largest shift in the biological response network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, interaction-based framework for prioritizing hazardous VOC mixtures in indoor environments, moving beyond concentration-based lists. It will generate testable data on specific synergistic interactions and propose a methodological shift from single-compant to mixture-network risk assessment for indoor air.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the ecological concept of 'keystone species' and network perturbation analysis to indoor air chemistry and toxicology, a direct analogy not previously explored. This moves beyond additive mixture models to identify emergent properties of chemical combinations.

proposals.title: Assessing the Sufficiency of Indoor Ozone Guidelines for Sensitive Subpopulations: A Focus on Personal Oxidation Field Dynamics and Secondary Product Formation
proposals.motivation: Current indoor air quality guidelines for ozone are based on population-level respiratory effects, primarily derived from chamber studies. However, pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone reacts rapidly with skin lipids like squalene, generating a personal 'oxidation field' and secondary pollutants (e.g., aldehydes, organic acids) in the breathing zone. This localized chemistry may create a unique exposure microenvironment not captured by bulk air measurements, posing a potential risk to sensitive groups such as children and asthmatics who may have different activity patterns, skin surface area-to-volume ratios, or underlying inflammation.
proposals.hypothesis: For sensitive subpopulations (children, asthmatics), the localized concentration of ozone reaction products generated at the skin and clothing surface, driven by personal oxidation field dynamics, will exceed thresholds of concern even when ambient indoor ozone levels are below current guideline values.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled human exposure studies in a climate-controlled chamber with ozone levels at and below common guideline values (e.g., 20-40 ppb). Participants will include adults (control), adults with mild asthma, and children. Real-time measurements of ozone and key reaction products (e.g., 6-MHO, 4-OPA) will be made simultaneously in the bulk chamber air and within the subject's immediate breathing zone using miniaturized, wearable sampling inlets connected to proton-transfer-reaction mass spectrometry (PTR-MS). A validated multi-zone chemical model, incorporating subject-specific parameters (skin oil composition from swabs, clothing coverage, respiration rate), will be used to extrapolate findings and simulate a wider range of indoor scenarios and subpopulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, empirical evidence linking guideline-level ozone concentrations to personal exposure metrics of reactive secondary products for sensitive groups. It will advance the field of indoor air science by shifting the focus from static ambient concentrations to dynamic, person-specific exposure. The results will inform potential revisions to risk assessment frameworks to better account for personal chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While the personal oxidation field is known, its implications for guideline compliance have not been tested. This proposal uniquely integrates real-time personal exposure monitoring of secondary products with physiological and demographic variables to explicitly challenge the protective adequacy of static, bulk-air guidelines for vulnerable individuals.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Screening Platform for Predicting VOC Emissions from Consumer Product Formulations Based on Constituent Chemical Libraries
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that volatile organic compounds (VOCs) emitted from consumer products like cleaners and detergents react with indoor oxidants (e.g., ozone) to form secondary pollutants, impacting indoor air quality and human health. Current product-by-product emission testing is slow and cannot keep pace with the vast number of formulations. A proactive, predictive approach is needed to assess the potential indoor chemistry impacts of entire product categories efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: The VOC emission profile of a complex consumer product formulation can be accurately modeled as a predictable function of the emission profiles of its individual chemical constituents and their interactions, analogous to pharmaceutical excipient screening.
proposals.method: We will first build a high-throughput in vitro emission library for core constituents (e.g., SLES, ethanol, limonene) using headspace sampling coupled with TD-GC/MS under controlled temperature and humidity. This data will train a computational model using a Random Forest regression architecture, incorporating chemical descriptors (e.g., vapor pressure, functional groups) and formulation ratios to predict VOC emission factors and speciation for untested mixtures. The model's predictive power will be validated against measured emissions from a blinded set of commercially available laundry detergents and dish soaps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first predictive, constituent-based framework for assessing consumer product emissions, moving beyond descriptive single-product studies. It will deliver a publicly accessible library of constituent emission factors and an open-source predictive model, enabling regulators and manufacturers to screen formulations for high-emission potential before market release.
proposals.novelty: While pharmaceutical science screens excipients for biological activity, this adapts the paradigm to screen for physicochemical emission behavior. It uniquely integrates high-throughput experimental data on building-block chemicals with machine learning to predict complex mixture emissions, a significant advance over existing empirical or purely theoretical approaches.

proposals.title: Integrating Atmospheric Aging Chambers with Lung-on-a-Chip Models to Assess the Inhalation Toxicity of Ozone-Initiated Product Emissions
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Weschler's work, demonstrates that ozone-initiated chemistry on skin and with indoor product emissions generates complex, reactive mixtures of secondary pollutants. Standard high-throughput in vitro toxicology assays fail to capture these dynamic chemical transformations and the physiologically relevant responses of the human respiratory tract, leading to potentially significant underestimation of inhalation risk from everyday consumer products.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tiered screening platform, which first subjects product volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions to realistic atmospheric aging (e.g., with ozone) and then exposes advanced lung microphysiological systems to the resulting mixture, will identify significantly greater inflammatory and oxidative stress responses than testing against static, parent VOCs alone.
proposals.method: The platform will employ a three-tiered design. Tier 1 uses high-throughput cell-free and simple cellular assays (e.g., A549 cells) for rapid screening of parent emissions. Tier 2 routes candidates to a controlled atmospheric aging chamber, where emissions from target personal care and cleaning products are mixed with physiologically relevant ozone concentrations (0-50 ppb) under controlled humidity and air flow, simulating indoor chemical transformation. Tier 3 introduces the aged mixture to a commercially available lung-on-a-chip model (e.g., Epithelix MucilAir or a similar co-culture system) that incorporates air-liquid interface, differentiated epithelial cells, and immune components (e.g., macrophages). Endpoints include cellular viability (MTT assay), inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8 via ELISA), and targeted oxidative stress markers (e.g., squalene ozonolysis products on skin surrogate surfaces).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, more predictive framework for screening the inhalation hazard of product emissions that accounts for real-world chemistry. It will generate the first hazard rankings for common indoor product categories based on the toxicity of their chemically aged emissions, directly informing indoor air quality and product safety assessments. The data will bridge the gap between atmospheric chemistry and modern toxicology.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct integration of a dynamic atmospheric reaction chamber, simulating indoor ozone chemistry, upstream of a sophisticated lung microphysiological system. This moves beyond static chemical dosing to replicate the evolving chemical exposure landscape that actually occurs in indoor environments, a critical flaw in existing screening paradigms.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Screening of Personal Care Product Emissions for Ozone-Induced Cellular Toxicity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone reacts with skin lipids like squalene and compounds in personal care products (PCPs) to generate a complex mixture of oxidation products (e.g., aldehydes, ketones) in the indoor environment and directly on the skin surface. The health impacts of this dynamic, personal exposure to reaction products are poorly characterized, as traditional chamber studies are slow and test few products. A rapid screening method is urgently needed to assess the toxic potential of emissions from the vast array of PCP formulations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-throughput in vitro toxicity assays will reveal significant and product-specific differences in the cytotoxicity and oxidative stress response induced by emissions from common PCPs upon reaction with ozone, correlating with specific product formulations and volatile organic compound (VOC) profiles.
proposals.method: We will adapt a tiered, high-throughput screening (HTS) approach from toxicology. First, a library of 50 common PCPs (lotions, creams, oils) will be applied to a standardized surface (glass, synthetic skin) in microplate wells and exposed to realistic indoor ozone levels (20-40 ppb) in a controlled flow system. Emitted gases will be directly delivered to cultured human lung (A549) and skin (HaCaT) cells in adjacent wells of a co-culture plate. Cellular responses will be assessed using rapid, automated in vitro assays: cell viability via resazurin reduction (Alamar Blue), and oxidative stress via a fluorogenic probe for reactive oxygen species (H2DCFDA). Dose-response curves will be generated for each product, and key VOC emissions will be characterized in parallel using TD-GC-MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comparative toxicity ranking of PCP emissions under realistic indoor ozone conditions, identifying product classes or ingredients of highest concern. It will establish a novel, efficient experimental pipeline for screening product-chemical interactions relevant to indoor air quality and personal exposure, moving beyond bulk chemistry to direct biological effect.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized the chemistry of ozone-skin/PCP reactions, applying high-throughput toxicological screening to this system to rapidly assess biological impact is novel. This approach bridges the gap between detailed analytical chemistry and slow, low-throughput health effects studies, enabling a new paradigm for risk prioritization.

proposals.title: Dynamic Exposure Patterns and Acute Health Responses: A Real-Time Sensor-Based Challenge to Indoor Air Guidelines for Asthmatic Children
proposals.motivation: Current indoor air quality guidelines for volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are based on long-term average concentrations and toxicological data from healthy adults, potentially overlooking acute effects in sensitive subpopulations. Pre-2026 research, including Weschler's work on ozone-skin chemistry and personal care products, demonstrates that personal exposure is highly dynamic and influenced by personal activities and chemistry. There is a critical lack of evidence linking these real-time, transient exposure peaks to acute physiological responses in vulnerable individuals, leaving guideline sufficiency in doubt.
proposals.hypothesis: Real-time, high-resolution monitoring will reveal that transient peaks of total VOCs and specific ozone-initiated reaction products (e.g., squalene oxidation products) in homes of asthmatic children correlate with measurable, acute changes in respiratory function and inflammatory biomarkers, even when 24-hour average concentrations remain below existing guideline values.
proposals.method: We will deploy dense networks of calibrated, low-cost metal-oxide semiconductor (MOS) and electrochemical VOC/ozone sensors in the bedrooms and main living areas of 30 homes with asthmatic children. Continuous data streams will be processed using a sliding-window peak detection algorithm to identify exposure events. These temporal patterns will be synchronized with concurrent data from wearable spirometers (FEV1, PEF) and periodic measurement of urinary leukotriene E4. Time-series regression models will analyze lagged associations between sensor-identified exposure peaks and acute health metrics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, temporally-resolved evidence linking dynamic indoor VOC exposures to acute health endpoints in a sensitive population. It will generate a novel dataset to critique the protective value of static, average-based guidelines and propose a framework for evaluating person-centric, peak-based exposure metrics for future standard setting.
proposals.novelty: While low-cost sensors have been used for spatial mapping, their application here is novel for capturing the *temporal dynamics of personal exposure* and directly challenging health-based guidelines. The fusion of real-time sensor data with high-frequency health monitoring in a real-world setting moves beyond compliance testing to a mechanistic, exposure-response investigation.

proposals.title: Lagrangian Particle Tracking for High-Resolution Modeling of Indoor VOC Dispersion and Human Exposure
proposals.motivation: Indoor VOC concentrations are highly dynamic, influenced by complex source dynamics, airflow, and human activity. Current models often use Eulerian approaches that smooth concentration gradients, failing to capture the fine-scale, transient plumes that critically determine personal exposure, especially for reactive species like ozone-squalene reaction products. A method to resolve these sharp gradients is needed to accurately assess inhalation and dermal uptake risks.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a Lagrangian particle tracking framework to indoor environments will successfully model the high spatiotemporal resolution dispersion of VOCs from episodic sources (e.g., personal care product use) and predict localized human exposure concentrations with greater fidelity than standard well-mixed or Eulerian CFD models.
proposals.method: We will use OpenFOAM to perform Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) simulations of airflow in a validated test chamber. VOC sources (e.g., squalene from skin oil, ethanol from a spray) will be modeled as emitters of inert Lagrangian particles, with release rates based on pre-2026 experimental data. Particle trajectories will be computed using a Runge-Kutta scheme, incorporating turbulent dispersion via a stochastic random-walk model. We will explicitly model a moving human manikin to perturb the flow field and act as a sink for reactive particles, simulating surface deposition and ozonolysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational tool for predicting personal exposure 'hotspots' in indoor spaces. It will advance fundamental understanding of how human movement and source location interact to drive inhalation exposure to both primary VOCs and secondary oxidation products. The model will be a valuable tool for designing interventions to mitigate peak exposures.
proposals.novelty: While Lagrangian methods are standard in atmospheric science, their application to model VOC dispersion at the room-to-human scale, explicitly integrating a moving human body as both a flow obstacle and a reactive sink, is a novel and non-obvious adaptation for indoor air chemistry.

proposals.title: Computational Screening of Personal Care Product Ingredients: Predicting Susceptibility to Indoor Ozonolysis via Molecular Docking
proposals.motivation: Indoor ozone reacts with skin lipids like squalene and ingredients from personal care products (PCPs), generating secondary pollutants that impact indoor air quality and human health. Current models often treat these reactions in bulk, lacking the molecular specificity to predict which exact chemical structures in complex PCP formulations are the most reactive. A predictive, high-throughput method is needed to identify key reactive ingredients before they are introduced into indoor environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Molecular docking methods, adapted from computational drug discovery, can reliably predict the binding geometries and interaction energies between ozone (or its Criegee intermediate) and specific organic molecules found in PCPs, thereby identifying molecular features that confer high susceptibility to ozonolysis.
proposals.method: We will use the AutoDock Vina software suite, a standard tool in biomolecular docking, to perform virtual screening. A library of 3D molecular structures of common PCP ingredients (e.g., squalene derivatives, terpenes, unsaturated esters) will be prepared using Open Babel. Ozone and a model Criegee intermediate will be defined as rigid 'targets'. Docking simulations will compute binding affinities (kcal/mol) and identify preferred binding sites (e.g., specific C=C bonds). Promising candidates will be validated with higher-level quantum chemical calculations (DFT) using Gaussian 16 to confirm reaction pathways and energy barriers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind ranked list of PCP ingredients by their predicted ozone reactivity, moving beyond bulk parameters to structure-activity relationships. It will provide a rapid screening tool for formulators and indoor chemists to preemptively identify ingredients likely to generate significant indoor oxidation products, linking molecular structure to potential indoor air quality impacts.
proposals.novelty: While molecular docking is ubiquitous in biochemistry, its application to atmospheric and indoor gas-phase oxidation chemistry is novel. This approach transplants a high-throughput virtual screening paradigm from one field to another, offering a new way to scale quantum chemical insights for practical environmental chemistry problems.

proposals.title: Linking Product Life Cycles to Indoor Air Quality and Personal Exposure: A Spatially-Explicit Urban Framework for Ozone-Skin Chemistry Derived Pollutants
proposals.motivation: Life Cycle Assessments (LCAs) of consumer products typically report aggregate emissions, failing to capture how use-phase pollutants directly impact indoor air quality and human exposure in specific urban settings. Pre-2026 research, including by Weschler, has established that ozone-initiated chemistry with skin lipids (e.g., squalene) and ingredients in personal care products generates secondary organic aerosols and other pollutants indoors. This creates a critical gap: the health burden from a product's life cycle is not just a function of its manufacturing emissions, but also of where and how it is used, given local housing conditions and occupant demographics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating product-specific LCA inventory data with spatially-resolved models of urban housing stock, indoor air chemistry, and product usage will reveal significant intra-urban disparities in the population exposure burden attributable to the use-phase of common consumer goods, particularly those involving ozone-reactive compounds.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel Urban Life Cycle Exposure Assessment (ULCEA) model by fusing: 1) LCA inventory data (e.g., from Ecoinvent) for key product categories (cleaning agents, personal care products), 2) city-scale housing stock models (e.g., from the U.S. Residential Energy Consumption Survey) to define building air exchange rates and indoor surface-to-volume ratios, 3) spatially-allocated product sales data, and 4) a modified indoor chemical box model (based on the INDCM framework) to simulate ozone-surface reactions and secondary pollutant formation. Exposure will be estimated by coupling modeled indoor concentrations with time-activity patterns from the Consolidated Human Activity Database (CHAD), using a Monte Carlo approach to propagate uncertainty across demographic subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for mapping the total environmental footprint of consumer goods onto specific urban populations, with high resolution for use-phase chemical transformations. It will deliver a novel 'exposure-to-burden' metric that identifies which subpopulations bear the greatest health risk from life cycle pollutants, directly informing targeted urban policy for public health and environmental justice.
proposals.novelty: While LCA and indoor chemistry are mature fields, this proposal's novelty lies in their explicit integration at an urban scale to assess personal exposure, moving beyond aggregate impacts or generic indoor models to account for the dynamic interplay between consumer product use, building science, and human oxidative chemistry.

proposals.title: Emissions and Indoor Chemistry of Volatile Terpenes from Natural Cleaning Products: A Combined Chamber and Human Exposure Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Weschler and others has established that ozone-initiated chemistry with skin lipids like squalene generates secondary pollutants that impact indoor air quality and personal exposure. Concurrently, consumer demand has driven a surge in 'green' cleaning products, which often contain high concentrations of reactive monoterpenes (e.g., limonene, α-pinene) as fragrances or solvents. The emissions of these terpenes and their subsequent reaction pathways with indoor oxidants, particularly in proximity to the human body where ozone concentrations are modified by the personal cloud, remain poorly characterized for this product class.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the use of natural cleaning products leads to significant emissions of reactive monoterpenes, which will rapidly react with ambient ozone to generate a suite of gas- and particle-phase secondary products, and that the spatial heterogeneity of these reactions—especially near the human body—will lead to higher personal exposure to these oxidation products than predicted by bulk room measurements.
proposals.method: The study will employ a three-part approach: 1) Chamber experiments to quantify primary VOC emissions from a representative set of natural cleaning products using Proton-Transfer-Reaction Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometry (PTR-ToF-MS). 2) Controlled environmental chamber studies introducing ozone to measure secondary product formation (e.g., formaldehyde, other carbonyls, ultrafine particles) via appropriate analytical instruments (e.g., HPLC, SMPS). 3) Human exposure studies in a simulated residential room, where a participant will perform light cleaning tasks. We will measure spatial gradients of terpenes, ozone, and oxidation products using a multi-port sampling system, comparing concentrations in the bulk room, the breathing zone, and near the skin surface, leveraging Weschler's established methods for studying the human oxidation field.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative link between emissions from a specific class of consumer products ('green' cleaners) and personal exposure to their secondary reaction products in an indoor setting. It will yield emission factors for key terpenes and their associated secondary yields, directly informing indoor air quality models. The findings will critically evaluate the 'green' product claim in the context of indoor chemistry and occupant health.
proposals.novelty: While terpene emissions from conventional cleaners are known, this study uniquely focuses on the 'natural' product market segment and integrates product emission testing with human-centric exposure assessment, explicitly probing the interaction between the product-derived oxidant demand and the personal ozone depletion field generated by skin lipids.

ideas.title: Use Lagrangian Particle Tracking from Atmospheric Science to Model VOC Dispersion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Lagrangian particle tracking method from atmospheric pollutant dispersion modeling to the indoor air quality context. The analogous solution involves releasing and tracking virtual particles within a CFD-simulated flow field to model the spread of emissions from specific sources. Adapted here, computational fluid dynamics is used to simulate room-scale airflow, but is augmented by defining indoor VOC sources (e.g., a spray cleaner, off-gassing furniture) as Lagrangian particle emitters. By tracking the trajectories and fates of these VOC packets over time, the model can predict highly detailed spatial and temporal concentration gradients, accounting for complex interactions like short-term puff dispersion, surface interactions, and the impact of human movement on local air mixing.

ideas.title: Molecular Docking for Atmospheric Chemistry: Predicting Ozone-Ingredient Binding Affinities
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from computational biochemistry—specifically, molecular docking simulations used in drug discovery—to the problem of studying quantum chemical reactions between product ingredients and atmospheric ozone. In the analogous field, docking predicts how a pharmaceutical ligand binds to a biological target. Here, we reconceptualize the ingredient as the 'ligand' and ozone (or its reactive derivatives) as the 'target.' The adapted idea involves using quantum chemistry software to perform virtual high-throughput screening of molecular ingredient libraries. By computationally docking these ingredients with ozone, we can predict binding geometries, interaction energies, and the most probable reactive sites, scaling down from bulk atmospheric models to precise, molecular-level predictions of which specific ingredient structures are most susceptible to ozonolysis or oxidation, thereby identifying potential sources of secondary pollutants or product degradation.

ideas.title: Tiered Physiologically-Relevant Screening Platform for Inhalation Toxicity of Product Emissions
ideas.description: This redesigned approach creates a multi-tiered testing strategy to overcome the biological oversimplification of standard high-throughput toxicology assays. It begins with rapid in vitro screening but immediately routes candidates to more complex microphysiological systems that replicate key aspects of the human respiratory tract, including air-flow, tissue barriers, and immune components. To address the flaw of static chemical testing, the system incorporates atmospheric aging chambers to simulate real-world chemical transformation of emissions before they are introduced to the biological models. The final output is a robust hazard ranking based on cellular viability, inflammatory markers, and omics-derived signatures of systemic stress, providing a far more predictive assessment of inhalation risk from product emissions.

ideas.description: This research deploys dense networks of low-cost, real-time VOC sensors in the homes of sensitive subpopulations (e.g., children, asthmatics) to collect continuous exposure data. It uses this high-resolution temporal data to critically evaluate whether existing indoor air guideline values, which are based on average concentrations and healthy adults, actually prevent acute exacerbations or sub-clinical effects in these groups. The approach moves beyond snapshot testing to identify dynamic exposure patterns, peak events, and cumulative burdens that may be missed by compliance-focused monitoring, thereby generating evidence to support the development of more protective, person-centric air quality standards.

The analogous solution involves high-throughput in vitro assays and computational modeling of individual compounds. Adapted to consumer product emission testing, the research would develop a platform to screen the core chemical constituents (e.g., specific surfactants like SLES, solvents like ethanol, common fragrance compounds) found in laundry detergents, dish soaps, and other household products. By creating a library of emission profiles for these building blocks, researchers could model and predict the VOC emissions of untested final product formulations, enabling a more efficient and proactive assessment of a broader product category range based on compositional data.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Trophic Cascade Analysis to VOC Mixture Risk Assessment
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that formaldehyde is the primary health concern from VOC emissions by drawing an analogy from ecological trophic cascade analysis. In ecology, system risk is understood by studying network interactions and indirect effects, not just the top predator. The analogous solution involves mapping interaction networks through perturbation experiments. Adapted to VOC research, this means conducting controlled mixture experiments where VOC combinations are systematically varied in environmental chambers. The biological responses (e.g., from in vitro or in vivo models) are then analyzed using network models to identify which non-formaldehyde VOCs or specific mixtures trigger synergistic or disproportionately severe health effects, revealing the true 'keystone' chemical stressors in complex emissions.

ideas.title: City-Scale Exposure Modeling via Material Flow Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Industrial Ecology's Material Flow Analysis (MFA) to the problem of scaling up to city-level population exposure. MFA solves the analogous problem of quantifying aggregate material stocks in a system by combining inflow data, stock models, and decay functions. The adapted research idea uses product sales data as the inflow, housing stock and demographic models to allocate the product, and product-specific usage/lifetime models to estimate the dynamic city-scale 'stock-in-use.' This framework models the aggregate public health impact by providing a spatially and temporally resolved estimate of population exposure potential based on the mass or units of a product present in the urban environment.

ideas.title: Adapting a Toxicological Priority Index from Pharmaceutical Impurity Screening for VOC Risk Ranking
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from pharmaceutical impurity screening to the problem of evaluating many VOCs against health guidelines. In pharmaceuticals, the analogous problem of assessing numerous potentially genotoxic impurities is solved with a staged, threshold-based 'Toxicological Concern' approach (e.g., ICH M7), using QSAR screening and a Threshold of Toxicological Concern (TTC) to prioritize only high-risk impurities for detailed assessment. Adapted to environmental VOCs, the research would develop a tiered framework: 1) Use in silico toxicity prediction tools and existing hazard data to categorize VOCs by potential health concern. 2) Apply a generic screening value (like an air concentration TTC) to measured environmental concentrations. 3) Conduct detailed comparisons to established or proposed compound-specific health guidelines only for those VOCs that clear both the hazard and exposure screening thresholds. This creates a efficient, risk-based funnel to manage the analytical and toxicological workload, ensuring resources are focused on the most significant potential risks from complex VOC mixtures.

ideas.title: An agent-based exposure model integrating mobility, microenvironment, and biomarker validation for city-scale public health impact
ideas.description: This redesigned system moves beyond static sales and housing proxies to dynamically model population exposure. It integrates anonymized mobile device data, time-activity surveys, and hyperlocal environmental sensor networks to simulate individual movement and exposure across microenvironments throughout the day. The agent-based model is calibrated and validated by comparing its exposure predictions against measured biomarkers from a strategically sampled cohort within the city. This creates a robust, mechanistic link between external sources, personal exposure, internal dose, and estimated population health impact.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Gap: A Paired Chamber-Field Study to Validate Real-World Complexity Models
ideas.description: This research directly tests the assumption that real-world complexity eludes chambers by creating a rigorous, paired comparison. Advanced test chambers are programmed with explicit, stochastic models of the hypothesized real-world complexities. Identical monitoring systems are deployed in these instrumented chambers and in a cohort of real homes. The core analysis compares the distributions of key outcome metrics (e.g., peak concentrations, exposure integrals, decay rates) between the chamber simulations and field data to statistically quantify if, and by how much, the real-world data falls outside the chamber's simulated envelope. This determines whether the complexity gap is significant or if modern chambers can adequately emulate it.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Sensor-Assisted CFD for Dynamic VOC Field Reconstruction
ideas.description: This system combines a high-fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) model of room airflow with a network of low-cost VOC sensors deployed throughout the space. A data assimilation algorithm continuously ingests real-time sensor measurements to calibrate and correct the model's unknown parameters, such as the location and strength of human VOC sources. This creates a dynamically updated, high-resolution reconstruction of the complete 3D concentration field, which is more accurate than either the pure simulation or sparse sensor data alone. The approach explicitly overcomes the limitation of unknown source terms by fusing physics-based modeling with real-world observation.

ideas.title: Context-Aware, Calibration-Corrected Sensor Networks for Reliable Household VOC Exposure Profiling
ideas.description: This redesigned system deploys a network of multi-sensor pods in homes, each containing a low-cost VOC sensor array alongside environmental context sensors. To overcome inherent sensor inaccuracy, each pod is periodically calibrated against a co-located passive reference sampler analyzed via gold-standard laboratory methods. A cloud-based Bayesian inference model then processes the calibrated real-time stream, incorporating environmental data and user-reported activity metadata to disentangle mixed signals and quantify exposures to specific VOC groups. The output is a temporally resolved, speciated exposure profile with associated uncertainty, transforming raw sensor data into reliable public health information.

ideas.title: Employ a Distributed Sensor Calibration and Data Fusion Framework from Precision Agriculture
ideas.description: This idea adapts the sensor data fusion and calibration framework from precision agriculture to the problem of continuous VOC exposure monitoring in homes. In agriculture, networks of low-cost field sensors are calibrated against sparse, high-fidelity measurements to create accurate, real-time maps of soil conditions. The adapted solution deploys a hybrid sensor node in homes, pairing a low-cost, real-time VOC sensor with a collocated passive sampler for time-integrated collection. Machine learning models fuse the continuous sensor data with the periodic, high-accuracy lab analysis of the passive samplers. This creates a calibrated, real-time exposure map for a household, overcoming the accuracy limitations of low-cost sensors alone and providing a robust method for longitudinal environmental health studies.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Real-Time Life Cycle Assessment via Product-System Digital Twins
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for dynamic, real-time Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) by creating a digital twin of a product and its life cycle system. The system continuously ingests data from IoT sensors in the supply chain, product usage telemetry, and end-of-life tracking to update environmental impact calculations in real time. This enables scenario modeling, hotspot identification under actual (not assumed) conditions, and prescriptive recommendations for reducing impact during operation. The approach transforms LCA from a static design-phase tool into an active management system for ongoing environmental optimization.

ideas.title: Adapting High-Throughput Phenotypic Screening from Drug Discovery for Emission Toxicity Profiling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of high-content phenotypic screening (HCS) from pharmaceutical drug discovery to the problem of screening many products for cellular toxicity from emissions. In drug discovery, HCS uses automated microscopy and image analysis to rapidly assess the complex phenotypic effects of thousands of compounds on cultured cells. The adapted research idea applies this platform to create a multiplexed, high-throughput assay where cells are exposed to emissions from various products. Automated imaging would then simultaneously quantify multiple sub-lethal cellular stress endpoints (e.g., oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, nuclear integrity), providing a rich, phenotypic toxicity profile for each emission sample far more informative than a single viability readout.
